No one has reported any adverse affects from the drug in Thursday's recall, the company said in a notice posted on the Food and Drug Administration's website.
Losartan tablets in 25, 50 and 100 mg doses are being recalled by Camber. They were distributed nationwide.
Consumers should speak with their doctor to discuss the recall before they stop taking the drug, or if they have experienced any adverse effects that may be related to the drug.
The identifying NDC numbers in the recall are Losartan 25 mg 31722-700-90, 31722-700-05, 31722-700-10; Losartan 50 mg 31722-701-30, 31722-701-90, 31722-70-10; and Losartan 100 mg 31722-702-30, 31722-702-90, and 31722-702-10. See a more detailed list, with lot and expiry numbers, here.
Trace amounts of N-Nitroso N-Methyl 4-amino butyric acid were found as an impurity or contaminant in an active ingredient. That compound is a potential human carcinogen, according to the recall notice.
Other manufacturers have recalled losartan in recent months, but Thursday's recall is for a different potential contaminant. Those recalls were made over detection of N-nitrosodiethylamine.
Anyone with questions about the latest recall may call Camber Pharmaceuticals at 866-495-1995 from 9 a.m. to 5 p.m. ET on weekdays.

answer: Bevel and Emboss gives the layer a three-dimensional look, making it appear either poking out towards the viewer or sunk into the design. Contour works with this by allowing you to set the overall shape of the bevel. You can smooth out the contour using the anti-aliased checkbox, and use the range slider to set the overall coverage of the bevel that you want the specific contour to apply to.
context:
This menu will appear upon right-clicking over a layer or folder.
Blending Options: This contains all of the blending options available for the current layer. All blending options contained in this section are the same as the Blending Mode drop-down at the top of the layers panel. Find out more in this tutorial: Clover Image Editor: Layer Blend Mode Walkthrough
Bevel and Emboss: This gives the layer a three-dimensional look and feel of either above (poking out towards the viewer) or sunk-into (etched into) the design.
Contour: Contour will allow you to set the overall shape of the bevel. If you check the anti-aliased checkbox, it will smooth out the contour, and the range slider will allow you to set the overall coverage of the bevel that you want this specific contour to apply to.
Texture: Texture will allow you to add a “bump map” or texture to the layer style. A "bump map” makes the surface of the layer appear higher or lower. Kind of like a textured wall, only much more customizable. Here you can add a pattern to the text if you so desire.
Stroke: Stroke produces an outline effect around the layer that you are working on. You can choose this effect to be in line, inside, or outside the layer border. You can also change the size, fill type, blend mode and opacity of the Stroke.
Inner Shadow: Inner Shadow is used to expand depth in a layer. It is most often used on text when using the Bevel or Emboss options.
Inner Glow: Inner Glow adds a “glow” effect to the inside of the layer. You can use this to add depth to the layer.
Satin: The Satin effect creates a silk or satin look, it can also be used to add additional depth.
Color Overlay The Color Overlay effect lets you put an overlay over the currently selected layer. You can adjust the blend and opacity of this effect.
Gradient Overlay: The Gradient Overlay effect allows you to place a gradient over the currently selected layer. You can adjust the blend, opacity gradient intensity, style, angle, and size of this effect.
Pattern Overlay: The Pattern Overlay effect allows you to place a patterned effect over the currently selected layer. You can adjust the blend, opacity, pattern, and scale of this effect.
Outer Glow: The Outer Glow effect adds a “glow” effect outside of the current layer.
Drop Shadow: The Drop Shadow effect allows you to add a shadow underneath the layer to provide a three-dimensional effect to it.
Duplicate Layers: |Ctrl + J| This allows you to duplicate a layer.
Delete: Deletes the currently selected layer.
Rasterize: Rasterize converts the layer into a bitmap and assigns each pixel an RBG color value. When you rasterize a layer, you can no longer edit that layer.
Rasterize Layer Style: Rasterize Layer Style applies a layer style that you added as part of the layer so that you can add the same layer style differently and not replace the previous style even though it is the same effect.
Convert to Shape: Convert to Shape converts the text layers to an image. Once you do this, you will no longer be able to add text to this layer.
Merge Down: |Ctrl + G| Merge Down combines the currently selected layer with the layer below, making them one layer.
Merge Layers: This merges all the layers in one folder and turns them into one layer.

Pattern Overlay: The Pattern Overlay effect allows you to place a patterned effect over the currently selected layer. You can adjust the blend, opacity, pattern, and scale of this effect.
Outer Glow: The Outer Glow effect adds a “glow” effect outside of the current layer.
Drop Shadow: The Drop Shadow effect allows you to add a shadow underneath the layer to provide a three-dimensional effect to it.
Duplicate Layers: |Ctrl + J| This allows you to duplicate a layer.
Delete: Deletes the currently selected layer.
Rasterize: Rasterize converts the layer into a bitmap and assigns each pixel an RBG color value. When you rasterize a layer, you can no longer edit that layer.
Rasterize Layer Style: Rasterize Layer Style applies a layer style that you added as part of the layer so that you can add the same layer style differently and not replace the previous style even though it is the same effect.
Convert to Shape: Convert to Shape converts the text layers to an image. Once you do this, you will no longer be able to add text to this layer.
Merge Down: |Ctrl + G| Merge Down combines the currently selected layer with the layer below, making them one layer.
Merge Layers: This merges all the layers in one folder and turns them into one layer.
question: What does the Inner Shadow effect do in image editing?

answer: Inner Glow adds a glow effect to the inside of the layer and can be used to add depth, while Outer Glow adds a glow effect outside of the current layer.
context:
This menu will appear upon right-clicking over a layer or folder.
Blending Options: This contains all of the blending options available for the current layer. All blending options contained in this section are the same as the Blending Mode drop-down at the top of the layers panel. Find out more in this tutorial: Clover Image Editor: Layer Blend Mode Walkthrough
Bevel and Emboss: This gives the layer a three-dimensional look and feel of either above (poking out towards the viewer) or sunk-into (etched into) the design.
Contour: Contour will allow you to set the overall shape of the bevel. If you check the anti-aliased checkbox, it will smooth out the contour, and the range slider will allow you to set the overall coverage of the bevel that you want this specific contour to apply to.
Texture: Texture will allow you to add a “bump map” or texture to the layer style. A "bump map” makes the surface of the layer appear higher or lower. Kind of like a textured wall, only much more customizable. Here you can add a pattern to the text if you so desire.
Stroke: Stroke produces an outline effect around the layer that you are working on. You can choose this effect to be in line, inside, or outside the layer border. You can also change the size, fill type, blend mode and opacity of the Stroke.
Inner Shadow: Inner Shadow is used to expand depth in a layer. It is most often used on text when using the Bevel or Emboss options.
Inner Glow: Inner Glow adds a “glow” effect to the inside of the layer. You can use this to add depth to the layer.
Satin: The Satin effect creates a silk or satin look, it can also be used to add additional depth.

question: What are the key differences between Inner Glow, Outer Glow, and Drop Shadow effects in layer styling?

answer: There are several effects that can add depth to a layer. Bevel and Emboss gives a three-dimensional look, making elements appear to poke out or sink into the design. Inner Shadow expands depth in a layer and is often used with Bevel or Emboss on text. Inner Glow and Satin can also add depth - Inner Glow adds a glow effect to the inside of the layer, while Satin creates a silk-like look that can enhance depth. Additionally, Drop Shadow adds a shadow underneath the layer for a three-dimensional effect.

answer: Rasterizing a layer converts it into a bitmap and assigns each pixel an RGB color value - once rasterized, the layer can no longer be edited. In contrast, rasterizing a layer style applies the layer style as part of the layer, allowing you to add the same layer style differently without replacing the previous style.
context:
This menu will appear upon right-clicking over a layer or folder.
Blending Options: This contains all of the blending options available for the current layer. All blending options contained in this section are the same as the Blending Mode drop-down at the top of the layers panel. Find out more in this tutorial: Clover Image Editor: Layer Blend Mode Walkthrough
Bevel and Emboss: This gives the layer a three-dimensional look and feel of either above (poking out towards the viewer) or sunk-into (etched into) the design.
Contour: Contour will allow you to set the overall shape of the bevel. If you check the anti-aliased checkbox, it will smooth out the contour, and the range slider will allow you to set the overall coverage of the bevel that you want this specific contour to apply to.
Texture: Texture will allow you to add a “bump map” or texture to the layer style. A "bump map” makes the surface of the layer appear higher or lower. Kind of like a textured wall, only much more customizable. Here you can add a pattern to the text if you so desire.
Stroke: Stroke produces an outline effect around the layer that you are working on. You can choose this effect to be in line, inside, or outside the layer border. You can also change the size, fill type, blend mode and opacity of the Stroke.
Inner Shadow: Inner Shadow is used to expand depth in a layer. It is most often used on text when using the Bevel or Emboss options.
Inner Glow: Inner Glow adds a “glow” effect to the inside of the layer. You can use this to add depth to the layer.
Satin: The Satin effect creates a silk or satin look, it can also be used to add additional depth.
Color Overlay The Color Overlay effect lets you put an overlay over the currently selected layer. You can adjust the blend and opacity of this effect.
Gradient Overlay: The Gradient Overlay effect allows you to place a gradient over the currently selected layer. You can adjust the blend, opacity gradient intensity, style, angle, and size of this effect.
Pattern Overlay: The Pattern Overlay effect allows you to place a patterned effect over the currently selected layer. You can adjust the blend, opacity, pattern, and scale of this effect.
Outer Glow: The Outer Glow effect adds a “glow” effect outside of the current layer.
Drop Shadow: The Drop Shadow effect allows you to add a shadow underneath the layer to provide a three-dimensional effect to it.
Duplicate Layers: |Ctrl + J| This allows you to duplicate a layer.
Delete: Deletes the currently selected layer.
Rasterize: Rasterize converts the layer into a bitmap and assigns each pixel an RBG color value. When you rasterize a layer, you can no longer edit that layer.
Rasterize Layer Style: Rasterize Layer Style applies a layer style that you added as part of the layer so that you can add the same layer style differently and not replace the previous style even though it is the same effect.
Convert to Shape: Convert to Shape converts the text layers to an image. Once you do this, you will no longer be able to add text to this layer.
Merge Down: |Ctrl + G| Merge Down combines the currently selected layer with the layer below, making them one layer.
Merge Layers: This merges all the layers in one folder and turns them into one layer.

Stroke: Stroke produces an outline effect around the layer that you are working on. You can choose this effect to be in line, inside, or outside the layer border. You can also change the size, fill type, blend mode and opacity of the Stroke.
Inner Shadow: Inner Shadow is used to expand depth in a layer. It is most often used on text when using the Bevel or Emboss options.
Inner Glow: Inner Glow adds a “glow” effect to the inside of the layer. You can use this to add depth to the layer.
Satin: The Satin effect creates a silk or satin look, it can also be used to add additional depth.
Color Overlay The Color Overlay effect lets you put an overlay over the currently selected layer. You can adjust the blend and opacity of this effect.
Gradient Overlay: The Gradient Overlay effect allows you to place a gradient over the currently selected layer. You can adjust the blend, opacity gradient intensity, style, angle, and size of this effect.
Pattern Overlay: The Pattern Overlay effect allows you to place a patterned effect over the currently selected layer. You can adjust the blend, opacity, pattern, and scale of this effect.
Outer Glow: The Outer Glow effect adds a “glow” effect outside of the current layer.
Drop Shadow: The Drop Shadow effect allows you to add a shadow underneath the layer to provide a three-dimensional effect to it.
Duplicate Layers: |Ctrl + J| This allows you to duplicate a layer.
Delete: Deletes the currently selected layer.
Rasterize: Rasterize converts the layer into a bitmap and assigns each pixel an RBG color value. When you rasterize a layer, you can no longer edit that layer.
Rasterize Layer Style: Rasterize Layer Style applies a layer style that you added as part of the layer so that you can add the same layer style differently and not replace the previous style even though it is the same effect.
Convert to Shape: Convert to Shape converts the text layers to an image. Once you do this, you will no longer be able to add text to this layer.
Merge Down: |Ctrl + G| Merge Down combines the currently selected layer with the layer below, making them one layer.
Merge Layers: This merges all the layers in one folder and turns them into one layer.

Pattern Overlay: The Pattern Overlay effect allows you to place a patterned effect over the currently selected layer. You can adjust the blend, opacity, pattern, and scale of this effect.
Outer Glow: The Outer Glow effect adds a “glow” effect outside of the current layer.
Drop Shadow: The Drop Shadow effect allows you to add a shadow underneath the layer to provide a three-dimensional effect to it.
Duplicate Layers: |Ctrl + J| This allows you to duplicate a layer.
Delete: Deletes the currently selected layer.
Rasterize: Rasterize converts the layer into a bitmap and assigns each pixel an RBG color value. When you rasterize a layer, you can no longer edit that layer.
Rasterize Layer Style: Rasterize Layer Style applies a layer style that you added as part of the layer so that you can add the same layer style differently and not replace the previous style even though it is the same effect.
Convert to Shape: Convert to Shape converts the text layers to an image. Once you do this, you will no longer be able to add text to this layer.
Merge Down: |Ctrl + G| Merge Down combines the currently selected layer with the layer below, making them one layer.
Merge Layers: This merges all the layers in one folder and turns them into one layer.
question: Could you explain how rasterization affects layer editability, and what's the distinction between regular rasterization and rasterizing layer styles?

answer: When combining design elements, you have several options: Merge Down (Ctrl + G) combines the selected layer with the layer below into one layer. Merge Layers combines all layers within a folder into a single layer. Once layers are merged, they become one unified layer and can't be separated again.
context:
This menu will appear upon right-clicking over a layer or folder.
Blending Options: This contains all of the blending options available for the current layer. All blending options contained in this section are the same as the Blending Mode drop-down at the top of the layers panel. Find out more in this tutorial: Clover Image Editor: Layer Blend Mode Walkthrough
Bevel and Emboss: This gives the layer a three-dimensional look and feel of either above (poking out towards the viewer) or sunk-into (etched into) the design.
Contour: Contour will allow you to set the overall shape of the bevel. If you check the anti-aliased checkbox, it will smooth out the contour, and the range slider will allow you to set the overall coverage of the bevel that you want this specific contour to apply to.
Texture: Texture will allow you to add a “bump map” or texture to the layer style. A "bump map” makes the surface of the layer appear higher or lower. Kind of like a textured wall, only much more customizable. Here you can add a pattern to the text if you so desire.
Stroke: Stroke produces an outline effect around the layer that you are working on. You can choose this effect to be in line, inside, or outside the layer border. You can also change the size, fill type, blend mode and opacity of the Stroke.
Inner Shadow: Inner Shadow is used to expand depth in a layer. It is most often used on text when using the Bevel or Emboss options.
Inner Glow: Inner Glow adds a “glow” effect to the inside of the layer. You can use this to add depth to the layer.
Satin: The Satin effect creates a silk or satin look, it can also be used to add additional depth.

question: Can you explain how the Bevel and Emboss effect works with Inner Shadow?

answer: There are several effects that can add depth and 3D appearance: Bevel and Emboss gives a three-dimensional look of either popping out or sinking in; Inner Shadow helps expand depth, especially when used with Bevel and Emboss; Satin effect can add additional depth; and Drop Shadow adds a shadow underneath to create a three-dimensional effect. The Texture effect can also add depth by creating a bump map that makes the surface appear higher or lower in certain areas.

Merge Down: |Ctrl + G| Merge Down combines the currently selected layer with the layer below, making them one layer.
Merge Layers: This merges all the layers in one folder and turns them into one layer.
question: What's the difference between Inner Glow and Outer Glow effects?

answer: Inner Glow adds a glowing effect to the inside of the layer and can be used to add depth, while Outer Glow adds a glowing effect to the outside of the layer.
context:
This menu will appear upon right-clicking over a layer or folder.
Blending Options: This contains all of the blending options available for the current layer. All blending options contained in this section are the same as the Blending Mode drop-down at the top of the layers panel. Find out more in this tutorial: Clover Image Editor: Layer Blend Mode Walkthrough
Bevel and Emboss: This gives the layer a three-dimensional look and feel of either above (poking out towards the viewer) or sunk-into (etched into) the design.
Contour: Contour will allow you to set the overall shape of the bevel. If you check the anti-aliased checkbox, it will smooth out the contour, and the range slider will allow you to set the overall coverage of the bevel that you want this specific contour to apply to.
Texture: Texture will allow you to add a “bump map” or texture to the layer style. A "bump map” makes the surface of the layer appear higher or lower. Kind of like a textured wall, only much more customizable. Here you can add a pattern to the text if you so desire.
Stroke: Stroke produces an outline effect around the layer that you are working on. You can choose this effect to be in line, inside, or outside the layer border. You can also change the size, fill type, blend mode and opacity of the Stroke.
Inner Shadow: Inner Shadow is used to expand depth in a layer. It is most often used on text when using the Bevel or Emboss options.
Inner Glow: Inner Glow adds a “glow” effect to the inside of the layer. You can use this to add depth to the layer.
Satin: The Satin effect creates a silk or satin look, it can also be used to add additional depth.
Color Overlay The Color Overlay effect lets you put an overlay over the currently selected layer. You can adjust the blend and opacity of this effect.
Gradient Overlay: The Gradient Overlay effect allows you to place a gradient over the currently selected layer. You can adjust the blend, opacity gradient intensity, style, angle, and size of this effect.
Pattern Overlay: The Pattern Overlay effect allows you to place a patterned effect over the currently selected layer. You can adjust the blend, opacity, pattern, and scale of this effect.
Outer Glow: The Outer Glow effect adds a “glow” effect outside of the current layer.
Drop Shadow: The Drop Shadow effect allows you to add a shadow underneath the layer to provide a three-dimensional effect to it.
Duplicate Layers: |Ctrl + J| This allows you to duplicate a layer.
Delete: Deletes the currently selected layer.
Rasterize: Rasterize converts the layer into a bitmap and assigns each pixel an RBG color value. When you rasterize a layer, you can no longer edit that layer.
Rasterize Layer Style: Rasterize Layer Style applies a layer style that you added as part of the layer so that you can add the same layer style differently and not replace the previous style even though it is the same effect.
Convert to Shape: Convert to Shape converts the text layers to an image. Once you do this, you will no longer be able to add text to this layer.
Merge Down: |Ctrl + G| Merge Down combines the currently selected layer with the layer below, making them one layer.
Merge Layers: This merges all the layers in one folder and turns them into one layer.

question: What are the key differences between Merge Down and Merge Layers commands?

answer: When combining layers, you have several options: Merge Down (Ctrl + G) combines the selected layer with the layer directly below it into one single layer. Merge Layers combines all layers within a folder into one layer. When layers are merged, they become a single layer that can't be separated again. You can also duplicate layers using Ctrl + J before merging to keep a backup of the original layers.

Contour: Contour will allow you to set the overall shape of the bevel. If you check the anti-aliased checkbox, it will smooth out the contour, and the range slider will allow you to set the overall coverage of the bevel that you want this specific contour to apply to.
Texture: Texture will allow you to add a “bump map” or texture to the layer style. A "bump map” makes the surface of the layer appear higher or lower. Kind of like a textured wall, only much more customizable. Here you can add a pattern to the text if you so desire.
Stroke: Stroke produces an outline effect around the layer that you are working on. You can choose this effect to be in line, inside, or outside the layer border. You can also change the size, fill type, blend mode and opacity of the Stroke.
Inner Shadow: Inner Shadow is used to expand depth in a layer. It is most often used on text when using the Bevel or Emboss options.
Inner Glow: Inner Glow adds a “glow” effect to the inside of the layer. You can use this to add depth to the layer.
Satin: The Satin effect creates a silk or satin look, it can also be used to add additional depth.
Color Overlay The Color Overlay effect lets you put an overlay over the currently selected layer. You can adjust the blend and opacity of this effect.
Gradient Overlay: The Gradient Overlay effect allows you to place a gradient over the currently selected layer. You can adjust the blend, opacity gradient intensity, style, angle, and size of this effect.
Pattern Overlay: The Pattern Overlay effect allows you to place a patterned effect over the currently selected layer. You can adjust the blend, opacity, pattern, and scale of this effect.
Outer Glow: The Outer Glow effect adds a “glow” effect outside of the current layer.
Drop Shadow: The Drop Shadow effect allows you to add a shadow underneath the layer to provide a three-dimensional effect to it.
Duplicate Layers: |Ctrl + J| This allows you to duplicate a layer.
Delete: Deletes the currently selected layer.
Rasterize: Rasterize converts the layer into a bitmap and assigns each pixel an RBG color value. When you rasterize a layer, you can no longer edit that layer.
Rasterize Layer Style: Rasterize Layer Style applies a layer style that you added as part of the layer so that you can add the same layer style differently and not replace the previous style even though it is the same effect.
Convert to Shape: Convert to Shape converts the text layers to an image. Once you do this, you will no longer be able to add text to this layer.
Merge Down: |Ctrl + G| Merge Down combines the currently selected layer with the layer below, making them one layer.
Merge Layers: This merges all the layers in one folder and turns them into one layer.
question: What is the functional difference between Rasterize Layer and Rasterize Layer Style commands?

answer: A Drop Shadow effect adds a shadow underneath the layer to create a three-dimensional effect, while Outer Glow adds a glowing effect outside of the layer. For inner effects, you can use Inner Glow which adds a glow to the inside of the layer to add depth.

question: What's the difference between adding a regular shadow and a glowing effect around my design?

answer: The Statewide Opioid Reporting Directive (SWORD) in Connecticut works by having hospitals and EMS crews report suspected opioid overdoses to the Connecticut Poison Control Center. Specialists then map this data using a federal web-based tracking application. This creates an early warning system that helps identify high-risk areas and overdose spikes, allowing local, state and federal agencies to quickly respond and direct their services to neighborhoods that need them most. The program has helped agencies distribute their limited resources more effectively since its debut in June 2019.
context:
Connecting state and local government leaders
A statewide project in Connecticut takes reports of suspected opioid overdoses from first responders and maps them in real time, helping community partners better direct their services to high-risk areas.
First responders in Connecticut reported 4,505 suspected opioid overdoses and 131 spikes in cases during the first year of a statewide reporting program, according to an annual report from the Connecticut Department of Public Health.
The initiative—known as the Statewide Opioid Reporting Directive, or SWORD—aims to track suspected overdoses in real time, creating an early warning system that pinpoints high-risk areas to help community partners direct their services to the neighborhoods that need them most. The system, which debuted in June 2019, is so far helping agencies distribute their limited resources more effectively, said Peter Canning, EMS coordinator for the University of Connecticut’s John Dempsey Hospital.
“For the first time, we have near real-time overdose data directly reported by first responders that enables us to act quickly to respond to overdose spikes and bad batches, resulting in lives saved,” he said in a statement. “The data is not only providing near real-time actionable intelligence for our public health efforts, but detailed data to help us better understand how to fight the ever-changing opioid crisis.”
The program, created by a 2018 state law, is an expansion of a pilot that began as a way to improve surveillance of opioid overdoses in the city of Hartford. The current project, a collaboration between state and local health and safety officials, starts by having hospitals and EMS crews to report all suspected opioid overdoses to the Connecticut Poison Control Center. Specialists there plug the data onto a map via a federal web-based tracking application, which allows “local, state and federal public health and public safety agencies to rapidly respond to overdoses in their respective communities,” the report said.
In the project’s first year, from June 2019 through May 2020, Hartford County recorded 1,590 suspected overdoses, the most in the state, followed by New Haven County (1,006) and Fairfield County (831). EMS crews reported 4,505 suspected overdoses but just 337 fatalities, far less than the actual number of drug-related deaths throughout Connecticut (there were 531 overdose deaths in just the first six months of 2020, according to data from the state health department).
Underreporting of fatal overdoses is a known and expected issue, according to the report, “because without (drug) paraphernalia or eyewitness accounts, EMS has no way to determine cause of death. Additionally, patients may pass away later at the hospital after the call has already been reported as nonfatal.”
Compliance is also less than 100% among EMS agencies. Only 17 of the 27 agencies asked to participate in the project routinely submit data, and compliance among those 17 topped out at 74% in February before falling to 62% in May.
“This lack of compliance reporting creates limitations in the representativeness of the data and knowledge gaps across the state, as well as risks associated with delayed detection of spikes,” the report said.
But any amount of data is useful in targeting resources to combat the opioid epidemic, officials said. For example, data collected by first responders showed that males accounted for 74% of overdoses in the state, and people between the ages of 25 and 39 were most likely to overdose. In cases where the “drug of exposure” was known, the vast majority of overdoses (87%) were due to heroin or fentanyl. Those details provide a crucial framework for first responders and community programs to work from, said Dr. Richard Karmin, EMS program director at UConn Health.
“The SWORD data enables us to identify overdose hotspots so we can better target our outreach, education, and naloxone training efforts,” he said in a statement. “The demographic information about who is overdosing provides us with actionable data on who is most at risk for overdose so we can better target our limited resources.”
Kate Elizabeth Queram is a staff correspondent with Route Fifty and is based in Washington, D.C.

The current project, a collaboration between state and local health and safety officials, starts by having hospitals and EMS crews to report all suspected opioid overdoses to the Connecticut Poison Control Center. Specialists there plug the data onto a map via a federal web-based tracking application, which allows “local, state and federal public health and public safety agencies to rapidly respond to overdoses in their respective communities,” the report said.
In the project’s first year, from June 2019 through May 2020, Hartford County recorded 1,590 suspected overdoses, the most in the state, followed by New Haven County (1,006) and Fairfield County (831). EMS crews reported 4,505 suspected overdoses but just 337 fatalities, far less than the actual number of drug-related deaths throughout Connecticut (there were 531 overdose deaths in just the first six months of 2020, according to data from the state health department).
Underreporting of fatal overdoses is a known and expected issue, according to the report, “because without (drug) paraphernalia or eyewitness accounts, EMS has no way to determine cause of death. Additionally, patients may pass away later at the hospital after the call has already been reported as nonfatal.”
Compliance is also less than 100% among EMS agencies. Only 17 of the 27 agencies asked to participate in the project routinely submit data, and compliance among those 17 topped out at 74% in February before falling to 62% in May.
“This lack of compliance reporting creates limitations in the representativeness of the data and knowledge gaps across the state, as well as risks associated with delayed detection of spikes,” the report said.
But any amount of data is useful in targeting resources to combat the opioid epidemic, officials said. For example, data collected by first responders showed that males accounted for 74% of overdoses in the state, and people between the ages of 25 and 39 were most likely to overdose. In cases where the “drug of exposure” was known, the vast majority of overdoses (87%) were due to heroin or fentanyl. Those details provide a crucial framework for first responders and community programs to work from, said Dr. Richard Karmin, EMS program director at UConn Health.
“The SWORD data enables us to identify overdose hotspots so we can better target our outreach, education, and naloxone training efforts,” he said in a statement. “The demographic information about who is overdosing provides us with actionable data on who is most at risk for overdose so we can better target our limited resources.”
Kate Elizabeth Queram is a staff correspondent with Route Fifty and is based in Washington, D.C.

“For the first time, we have near real-time overdose data directly reported by first responders that enables us to act quickly to respond to overdose spikes and bad batches, resulting in lives saved,” he said in a statement. “The data is not only providing near real-time actionable intelligence for our public health efforts, but detailed data to help us better understand how to fight the ever-changing opioid crisis.”
The program, created by a 2018 state law, is an expansion of a pilot that began as a way to improve surveillance of opioid overdoses in the city of Hartford. The current project, a collaboration between state and local health and safety officials, starts by having hospitals and EMS crews to report all suspected opioid overdoses to the Connecticut Poison Control Center. Specialists there plug the data onto a map via a federal web-based tracking application, which allows “local, state and federal public health and public safety agencies to rapidly respond to overdoses in their respective communities,” the report said.
In the project’s first year, from June 2019 through May 2020, Hartford County recorded 1,590 suspected overdoses, the most in the state, followed by New Haven County (1,006) and Fairfield County (831). EMS crews reported 4,505 suspected overdoses but just 337 fatalities, far less than the actual number of drug-related deaths throughout Connecticut (there were 531 overdose deaths in just the first six months of 2020, according to data from the state health department).
Underreporting of fatal overdoses is a known and expected issue, according to the report, “because without (drug) paraphernalia or eyewitness accounts, EMS has no way to determine cause of death. Additionally, patients may pass away later at the hospital after the call has already been reported as nonfatal.”
Compliance is also less than 100% among EMS agencies. Only 17 of the 27 agencies asked to participate in the project routinely submit data, and compliance among those 17 topped out at 74% in February before falling to 62% in May.
“This lack of compliance reporting creates limitations in the representativeness of the data and knowledge gaps across the state, as well as risks associated with delayed detection of spikes,” the report said.
But any amount of data is useful in targeting resources to combat the opioid epidemic, officials said. For example, data collected by first responders showed that males accounted for 74% of overdoses in the state, and people between the ages of 25 and 39 were most likely to overdose. In cases where the “drug of exposure” was known, the vast majority of overdoses (87%) were due to heroin or fentanyl. Those details provide a crucial framework for first responders and community programs to work from, said Dr. Richard Karmin, EMS program director at UConn Health.
“The SWORD data enables us to identify overdose hotspots so we can better target our outreach, education, and naloxone training efforts,” he said in a statement. “The demographic information about who is overdosing provides us with actionable data on who is most at risk for overdose so we can better target our limited resources.”
Kate Elizabeth Queram is a staff correspondent with Route Fifty and is based in Washington, D.C.

The initiative—known as the Statewide Opioid Reporting Directive, or SWORD—aims to track suspected overdoses in real time, creating an early warning system that pinpoints high-risk areas to help community partners direct their services to the neighborhoods that need them most. The system, which debuted in June 2019, is so far helping agencies distribute their limited resources more effectively, said Peter Canning, EMS coordinator for the University of Connecticut’s John Dempsey Hospital.
“For the first time, we have near real-time overdose data directly reported by first responders that enables us to act quickly to respond to overdose spikes and bad batches, resulting in lives saved,” he said in a statement. “The data is not only providing near real-time actionable intelligence for our public health efforts, but detailed data to help us better understand how to fight the ever-changing opioid crisis.”
The program, created by a 2018 state law, is an expansion of a pilot that began as a way to improve surveillance of opioid overdoses in the city of Hartford. The current project, a collaboration between state and local health and safety officials, starts by having hospitals and EMS crews to report all suspected opioid overdoses to the Connecticut Poison Control Center. Specialists there plug the data onto a map via a federal web-based tracking application, which allows “local, state and federal public health and public safety agencies to rapidly respond to overdoses in their respective communities,” the report said.
In the project’s first year, from June 2019 through May 2020, Hartford County recorded 1,590 suspected overdoses, the most in the state, followed by New Haven County (1,006) and Fairfield County (831). EMS crews reported 4,505 suspected overdoses but just 337 fatalities, far less than the actual number of drug-related deaths throughout Connecticut (there were 531 overdose deaths in just the first six months of 2020, according to data from the state health department).
Underreporting of fatal overdoses is a known and expected issue, according to the report, “because without (drug) paraphernalia or eyewitness accounts, EMS has no way to determine cause of death. Additionally, patients may pass away later at the hospital after the call has already been reported as nonfatal.”
Compliance is also less than 100% among EMS agencies. Only 17 of the 27 agencies asked to participate in the project routinely submit data, and compliance among those 17 topped out at 74% in February before falling to 62% in May.
“This lack of compliance reporting creates limitations in the representativeness of the data and knowledge gaps across the state, as well as risks associated with delayed detection of spikes,” the report said.
But any amount of data is useful in targeting resources to combat the opioid epidemic, officials said. For example, data collected by first responders showed that males accounted for 74% of overdoses in the state, and people between the ages of 25 and 39 were most likely to overdose. In cases where the “drug of exposure” was known, the vast majority of overdoses (87%) were due to heroin or fentanyl. Those details provide a crucial framework for first responders and community programs to work from, said Dr. Richard Karmin, EMS program director at UConn Health.
“The SWORD data enables us to identify overdose hotspots so we can better target our outreach, education, and naloxone training efforts,” he said in a statement. “The demographic information about who is overdosing provides us with actionable data on who is most at risk for overdose so we can better target our limited resources.”
Kate Elizabeth Queram is a staff correspondent with Route Fifty and is based in Washington, D.C.

First responders in Connecticut reported 4,505 suspected opioid overdoses and 131 spikes in cases during the first year of a statewide reporting program, according to an annual report from the Connecticut Department of Public Health.
The initiative—known as the Statewide Opioid Reporting Directive, or SWORD—aims to track suspected overdoses in real time, creating an early warning system that pinpoints high-risk areas to help community partners direct their services to the neighborhoods that need them most. The system, which debuted in June 2019, is so far helping agencies distribute their limited resources more effectively, said Peter Canning, EMS coordinator for the University of Connecticut’s John Dempsey Hospital.
“For the first time, we have near real-time overdose data directly reported by first responders that enables us to act quickly to respond to overdose spikes and bad batches, resulting in lives saved,” he said in a statement. “The data is not only providing near real-time actionable intelligence for our public health efforts, but detailed data to help us better understand how to fight the ever-changing opioid crisis.”
The program, created by a 2018 state law, is an expansion of a pilot that began as a way to improve surveillance of opioid overdoses in the city of Hartford. The current project, a collaboration between state and local health and safety officials, starts by having hospitals and EMS crews to report all suspected opioid overdoses to the Connecticut Poison Control Center. Specialists there plug the data onto a map via a federal web-based tracking application, which allows “local, state and federal public health and public safety agencies to rapidly respond to overdoses in their respective communities,” the report said.
In the project’s first year, from June 2019 through May 2020, Hartford County recorded 1,590 suspected overdoses, the most in the state, followed by New Haven County (1,006) and Fairfield County (831). EMS crews reported 4,505 suspected overdoses but just 337 fatalities, far less than the actual number of drug-related deaths throughout Connecticut (there were 531 overdose deaths in just the first six months of 2020, according to data from the state health department).
Underreporting of fatal overdoses is a known and expected issue, according to the report, “because without (drug) paraphernalia or eyewitness accounts, EMS has no way to determine cause of death. Additionally, patients may pass away later at the hospital after the call has already been reported as nonfatal.”
Compliance is also less than 100% among EMS agencies. Only 17 of the 27 agencies asked to participate in the project routinely submit data, and compliance among those 17 topped out at 74% in February before falling to 62% in May.
“This lack of compliance reporting creates limitations in the representativeness of the data and knowledge gaps across the state, as well as risks associated with delayed detection of spikes,” the report said.
But any amount of data is useful in targeting resources to combat the opioid epidemic, officials said. For example, data collected by first responders showed that males accounted for 74% of overdoses in the state, and people between the ages of 25 and 39 were most likely to overdose. In cases where the “drug of exposure” was known, the vast majority of overdoses (87%) were due to heroin or fentanyl. Those details provide a crucial framework for first responders and community programs to work from, said Dr. Richard Karmin, EMS program director at UConn Health.
“The SWORD data enables us to identify overdose hotspots so we can better target our outreach, education, and naloxone training efforts,” he said in a statement. “The demographic information about who is overdosing provides us with actionable data on who is most at risk for overdose so we can better target our limited resources.”
Kate Elizabeth Queram is a staff correspondent with Route Fifty and is based in Washington, D.C.
question: How many suspected opioid overdoses were reported in Connecticut counties during the first year of SWORD?

answer: The SWORD system requires hospitals and EMS crews to report all suspected opioid overdoses to the Connecticut Poison Control Center. Specialists then map this data using a federal web-based tracking application, allowing local, state and federal agencies to rapidly respond to overdoses in their communities. The system, which began in June 2019, helps identify overdose hotspots and enables quick response to overdose spikes and dangerous drug batches.

question: How does the Statewide Opioid Reporting Directive (SWORD) system function to enable real-time overdose tracking in Connecticut?

answer: There are several challenges in accurately tracking fatal drug overdoses. First, there is underreporting of fatal overdoses because EMS crews have no way to determine cause of death without drug paraphernalia or eyewitness accounts. Additionally, patients may die later at the hospital after the incident has already been reported as nonfatal. There are also compliance issues, as only 17 out of 27 EMS agencies routinely submit data, and even among those participating, compliance peaked at 74% before dropping to 62%. This lack of complete reporting creates limitations in data representation and risks delayed detection of overdose spikes.

The program, which began in June 2019, helps agencies distribute their limited resources more effectively.
context:
Connecting state and local government leaders
A statewide project in Connecticut takes reports of suspected opioid overdoses from first responders and maps them in real time, helping community partners better direct their services to high-risk areas.
First responders in Connecticut reported 4,505 suspected opioid overdoses and 131 spikes in cases during the first year of a statewide reporting program, according to an annual report from the Connecticut Department of Public Health.
The initiative—known as the Statewide Opioid Reporting Directive, or SWORD—aims to track suspected overdoses in real time, creating an early warning system that pinpoints high-risk areas to help community partners direct their services to the neighborhoods that need them most. The system, which debuted in June 2019, is so far helping agencies distribute their limited resources more effectively, said Peter Canning, EMS coordinator for the University of Connecticut’s John Dempsey Hospital.
“For the first time, we have near real-time overdose data directly reported by first responders that enables us to act quickly to respond to overdose spikes and bad batches, resulting in lives saved,” he said in a statement. “The data is not only providing near real-time actionable intelligence for our public health efforts, but detailed data to help us better understand how to fight the ever-changing opioid crisis.”
The program, created by a 2018 state law, is an expansion of a pilot that began as a way to improve surveillance of opioid overdoses in the city of Hartford. The current project, a collaboration between state and local health and safety officials, starts by having hospitals and EMS crews to report all suspected opioid overdoses to the Connecticut Poison Control Center. Specialists there plug the data onto a map via a federal web-based tracking application, which allows “local, state and federal public health and public safety agencies to rapidly respond to overdoses in their respective communities,” the report said.
In the project’s first year, from June 2019 through May 2020, Hartford County recorded 1,590 suspected overdoses, the most in the state, followed by New Haven County (1,006) and Fairfield County (831). EMS crews reported 4,505 suspected overdoses but just 337 fatalities, far less than the actual number of drug-related deaths throughout Connecticut (there were 531 overdose deaths in just the first six months of 2020, according to data from the state health department).
Underreporting of fatal overdoses is a known and expected issue, according to the report, “because without (drug) paraphernalia or eyewitness accounts, EMS has no way to determine cause of death. Additionally, patients may pass away later at the hospital after the call has already been reported as nonfatal.”
Compliance is also less than 100% among EMS agencies. Only 17 of the 27 agencies asked to participate in the project routinely submit data, and compliance among those 17 topped out at 74% in February before falling to 62% in May.
“This lack of compliance reporting creates limitations in the representativeness of the data and knowledge gaps across the state, as well as risks associated with delayed detection of spikes,” the report said.
But any amount of data is useful in targeting resources to combat the opioid epidemic, officials said. For example, data collected by first responders showed that males accounted for 74% of overdoses in the state, and people between the ages of 25 and 39 were most likely to overdose. In cases where the “drug of exposure” was known, the vast majority of overdoses (87%) were due to heroin or fentanyl. Those details provide a crucial framework for first responders and community programs to work from, said Dr. Richard Karmin, EMS program director at UConn Health.
“The SWORD data enables us to identify overdose hotspots so we can better target our outreach, education, and naloxone training efforts,” he said in a statement. “The demographic information about who is overdosing provides us with actionable data on who is most at risk for overdose so we can better target our limited resources.”
Kate Elizabeth Queram is a staff correspondent with Route Fifty and is based in Washington, D.C.

question: What demographic patterns were identified in Connecticut's opioid overdose data through the SWORD program?

answer: There are two main challenges in getting accurate overdose data in Connecticut. First, there is underreporting of fatal overdoses because EMS crews often can't determine cause of death without drug paraphernalia or eyewitness accounts, and some patients die later at the hospital after the call has been reported as non-fatal. Second, there are compliance issues with data reporting - only 17 of 27 EMS agencies routinely submit data, and even among those agencies, compliance peaked at 74% in February before dropping to 62% in May. This creates limitations in data representation and risks delayed detection of overdose spikes.

“For the first time, we have near real-time overdose data directly reported by first responders that enables us to act quickly to respond to overdose spikes and bad batches, resulting in lives saved,” he said in a statement. “The data is not only providing near real-time actionable intelligence for our public health efforts, but detailed data to help us better understand how to fight the ever-changing opioid crisis.”
The program, created by a 2018 state law, is an expansion of a pilot that began as a way to improve surveillance of opioid overdoses in the city of Hartford. The current project, a collaboration between state and local health and safety officials, starts by having hospitals and EMS crews to report all suspected opioid overdoses to the Connecticut Poison Control Center. Specialists there plug the data onto a map via a federal web-based tracking application, which allows “local, state and federal public health and public safety agencies to rapidly respond to overdoses in their respective communities,” the report said.
In the project’s first year, from June 2019 through May 2020, Hartford County recorded 1,590 suspected overdoses, the most in the state, followed by New Haven County (1,006) and Fairfield County (831). EMS crews reported 4,505 suspected overdoses but just 337 fatalities, far less than the actual number of drug-related deaths throughout Connecticut (there were 531 overdose deaths in just the first six months of 2020, according to data from the state health department).
Underreporting of fatal overdoses is a known and expected issue, according to the report, “because without (drug) paraphernalia or eyewitness accounts, EMS has no way to determine cause of death. Additionally, patients may pass away later at the hospital after the call has already been reported as nonfatal.”
Compliance is also less than 100% among EMS agencies. Only 17 of the 27 agencies asked to participate in the project routinely submit data, and compliance among those 17 topped out at 74% in February before falling to 62% in May.
“This lack of compliance reporting creates limitations in the representativeness of the data and knowledge gaps across the state, as well as risks associated with delayed detection of spikes,” the report said.
But any amount of data is useful in targeting resources to combat the opioid epidemic, officials said. For example, data collected by first responders showed that males accounted for 74% of overdoses in the state, and people between the ages of 25 and 39 were most likely to overdose. In cases where the “drug of exposure” was known, the vast majority of overdoses (87%) were due to heroin or fentanyl. Those details provide a crucial framework for first responders and community programs to work from, said Dr. Richard Karmin, EMS program director at UConn Health.
“The SWORD data enables us to identify overdose hotspots so we can better target our outreach, education, and naloxone training efforts,” he said in a statement. “The demographic information about who is overdosing provides us with actionable data on who is most at risk for overdose so we can better target our limited resources.”
Kate Elizabeth Queram is a staff correspondent with Route Fifty and is based in Washington, D.C.

question: What is the SWORD program in Connecticut and what does it aim to do?

answer: During the first year (June 2019 through May 2020), there were 4,505 suspected opioid overdoses and 131 spikes reported. Hartford County had the most cases with 1,590 overdoses, followed by New Haven County (1,006) and Fairfield County (831). The data showed that males accounted for 74% of overdoses, and people aged 25-39 were most likely to overdose. In cases where the drug type was known, 87% of overdoses were due to heroin or fentanyl. While EMS crews reported 337 fatalities, this was known to be an undercount of actual overdose deaths.

question: What are the main findings about drug overdoses in Connecticut based on the tracking system's first year?

answer: The study employed a post test-only control group design with a sample of 63 students. The sample was heterogeneous to provide representation across ability level and gender. The achievement and retention were measured using researcher-made posttest and delayed-posttest instruments. Statistical analysis was conducted using Two-way analysis of Covariance (ANCOVA) with a significance level of 0.05.
context:
Tahir, Alyas Qadeer (2005) A COMPARATIVE STUDY OF THE EFFECT OF USE OF INFORMATION AND COMMUNICATION TECHNOLOGY IN VARIED TEACHING APPROACHES ON ACHIEVEMENT AND RETENTION OF STUDENTS OF MATHEMATICS. PhD thesis, Gomal University, D.I. Khan.
The purposed of this study was to compare and determine the more effective approach by used of information and communication technology (ICT) on achievement and retention of secondary school students of mathematics in Pakistan. The three approaches used for this study were, the computer based instruction(CBI), computer Based learning (CBL), and the teacher centered TC) approach. The research design was the post test-only control group design. Seventeen research questions were put in the study. Sample of the study were 63 students and was heterogeneous to provide representation across ability level and gender. The achievement and retention instruments were in the form of researchers, made posttedst and the delayed-posttest. The CBI and CBL boys and girls students (taught separately) were grouped as treatment group. The same topic of mathematics, ÃÂÃÂ¢ÃÂÃÂÃÂÃÂConcept of MatricesÃÂÃÂ¢ÃÂÃÂÃÂÃÂ was taught to all three groups. The CBI groups were taught through software-Educational Program of Gifted Youth (EPGY), developed by Stanford University of, USA. These groups were also supported by lectures, drill and practice and self-assessment opportunities. The CBL groups used the EPGY software and in addition they made sue of all possible resources of information and communication technology; internet, e-mail, chatting and on-line help. The TC groups were taught through traditional teaching techniques. Two-way analysis of Covariance (ANCOVA) procedure form SPSS program was adapted for the analysis of data of the study. Significant levels of all research hypothesis tested in this study were at the 0.05 levels. There was no significant difference among score of the students taught mathematics through CBI, CBL and TC approaches on achievement. The main effect of the groups and ability did not meet the 0.05 level of significance. Difference among the groupÃÂÃÂ¢ÃÂÃÂÃÂÃÂs retention of the students taught mathematics through CBI, CBL and TC approaches. The main effect of the ability did meet the 0.05 level of significance on delayed-posttest. The mian effect comparison of groups was also significant at 0.023. However, the main effect for group reach statistical significance on delayed-posttest. Post-hoc comparison using the Tukey HSD test indicated that the means score of delayed-posttest for the CBL group was significantly different from the TC group. The CBI group did not differ significantly from either of the CBL or TC groups. It was found that the CBL group had significantly scored higher in achieving as well as retaining the content of mathematics taught to them during the experiment. The blow average students retained significantly more than the average and above average students. The girls students overall scored considerably higher than the boys in delayed-posttest. It was concluded that the use of CBL approach in teaching of mathematics at secondary level in Pakistan can be encouraged for better achievement and retention of the subject which is one of the objective of teaching of mathematics at this stage.

question: How did student ability levels correlate with retention outcomes in the ICT-based mathematics teaching study, and what were the specific findings regarding below average students?

answer: The Computer Based Learning (CBL) approach was found to be most effective for retention. Post-hoc comparison using the Tukey HSD test showed that the CBL group's mean scores were significantly different from the Teacher Centered (TC) group, with CBL students scoring significantly higher in both achieving and retaining the mathematical content. The Computer Based Instruction (CBI) group did not differ significantly from either CBL or TC groups.

question: How did different student ability levels affect the retention of mathematical concepts in this study of teaching methods?

answer: The study employed a post test-only control group design with 17 research questions. Data analysis was conducted using Two-way analysis of Covariance (ANCOVA) procedure from SPSS program, with significance levels set at 0.05.

question: Does a student's initial ability level affect how well they remember what they learned?

answer: The study compared three different approaches: Computer Based Instruction (CBI), Computer Based Learning (CBL), and Teacher Centered (TC) approach. While there was no significant difference in immediate achievement scores among these three approaches, CBL proved more effective for long-term retention. CBL students used EPGY software (developed by Stanford University) plus additional technology resources like internet, e-mail, chatting and online help. CBI students used only the EPGY software with supporting lectures and practice, while TC groups used traditional teaching techniques. The CBL approach was recommended for teaching mathematics at the secondary level in Pakistan for better achievement and retention.

question: What were the comparative outcomes in mathematics retention between Computer-Based Learning (CBL) and traditional teacher-centered instruction based on empirical research?

answer: According to the research findings, students who used Computer Based Learning (CBL) scored significantly higher in both learning and retaining mathematics content compared to those taught through traditional teaching methods. question: What was the statistical significance level used for testing research hypotheses in this comparative study of teaching approaches?

answer: The study compared two technology-based approaches: Computer Based Learning (CBL) and Computer Based Instruction (CBI). The CBL groups used EPGY software (developed by Stanford University) along with additional ICT resources including internet, e-mail, chatting, and online help. The CBI groups only used the EPGY software supported by lectures, drill and practice, and self-assessment opportunities. The results showed that CBL approach, which utilized comprehensive digital resources, was more effective, particularly in terms of retention, compared to the more limited CBI approach.

The three approaches used for this study were, the computer based instruction(CBI), computer Based learning (CBL), and the teacher centered TC) approach. The research design was the post test-only control group design. Seventeen research questions were put in the study. Sample of the study were 63 students and was heterogeneous to provide representation across ability level and gender. The achievement and retention instruments were in the form of researchers, made posttedst and the delayed-posttest. The CBI and CBL boys and girls students (taught separately) were grouped as treatment group. The same topic of mathematics, ÃÂÃÂ¢ÃÂÃÂÃÂÃÂConcept of MatricesÃÂÃÂ¢ÃÂÃÂÃÂÃÂ was taught to all three groups. The CBI groups were taught through software-Educational Program of Gifted Youth (EPGY), developed by Stanford University of, USA. These groups were also supported by lectures, drill and practice and self-assessment opportunities. The CBL groups used the EPGY software and in addition they made sue of all possible resources of information and communication technology; internet, e-mail, chatting and on-line help. The TC groups were taught through traditional teaching techniques. Two-way analysis of Covariance (ANCOVA) procedure form SPSS program was adapted for the analysis of data of the study. Significant levels of all research hypothesis tested in this study were at the 0.05 levels. There was no significant difference among score of the students taught mathematics through CBI, CBL and TC approaches on achievement. The main effect of the groups and ability did not meet the 0.05 level of significance. Difference among the groupÃÂÃÂ¢ÃÂÃÂÃÂÃÂs retention of the students taught mathematics through CBI, CBL and TC approaches. The main effect of the ability did meet the 0.05 level of significance on delayed-posttest. The mian effect comparison of groups was also significant at 0.023. However, the main effect for group reach statistical significance on delayed-posttest. Post-hoc comparison using the Tukey HSD test indicated that the means score of delayed-posttest for the CBL group was significantly different from the TC group. The CBI group did not differ significantly from either of the CBL or TC groups. It was found that the CBL group had significantly scored higher in achieving as well as retaining the content of mathematics taught to them during the experiment. The blow average students retained significantly more than the average and above average students. The girls students overall scored considerably higher than the boys in delayed-posttest. It was concluded that the use of CBL approach in teaching of mathematics at secondary level in Pakistan can be encouraged for better achievement and retention of the subject which is one of the objective of teaching of mathematics at this stage.

question: In the evaluation of different pedagogical approaches for mathematics instruction, how did Computer Based Learning (CBL) compare to traditional teaching methods in terms of knowledge retention?

answer: The Computer Based Learning (CBL) group showed significantly better results compared to the Teacher Centered (TC) group, particularly in retention. Post-hoc comparison using the Tukey HSD test showed that the CBL group's delayed-posttest scores were significantly different from the TC group. The CBL group scored higher in both achieving and retaining the mathematical content taught during the experiment.

question: What were the main differences in learning outcomes between Computer Based Learning (CBL) and traditional teaching methods for mathematics students?

answer: The study found that below average students retained significantly more mathematical content than both average and above average students. question: How did student ability levels affect the retention of mathematics content in the study?

answer: The study revealed that female students overall scored considerably higher than male students in the delayed post-test, which measured retention of mathematical content. Both boys and girls were taught separately in the Computer Based Instruction (CBI) and Computer Based Learning (CBL) groups.

question: Is there any difference between how boys and girls perform in math when using technology-based learning?

answer: The study employed a post test-only control group design with 63 heterogeneous students representing different ability levels and genders. The research utilized two-way analysis of Covariance (ANCOVA) procedure from SPSS program for data analysis, with significance levels set at 0.05. Achievement and retention were measured through researcher-made posttest and delayed-posttest instruments. The study examined seventeen research questions, and post-hoc comparisons were conducted using the Tukey HSD test to determine specific group differences.

question: What were the comparative effects of Computer Based Learning (CBL) versus Teacher Centered (TC) approaches on mathematics retention according to the statistical analysis?

answer: While both CBL and CBI groups used the Educational Program of Gifted Youth (EPGY) software developed by Stanford University, the CBL groups had access to additional information and communication technology resources. Specifically, CBL groups utilized internet, e-mail, chatting, and on-line help, whereas CBI groups were only supported by lectures, drill and practice, and self-assessment opportunities.

question: How many students participated in this study comparing different teaching methods for mathematics?

answer: The study compared three different teaching approaches: Computer Based Instruction (CBI), Computer Based Learning (CBL), and Teacher Centered (TC) approach. question: What were the key differences in math learning outcomes between boys and girls when using technology-based teaching methods?

answer: According to the study results, while there was no significant difference in initial achievement between Computer Based Learning (CBL), Computer Based Instruction (CBI), and Teacher Centered (TC) approaches, there were differences in retention. The CBL group had significantly higher scores than the TC group in the delayed post-test, indicating better retention. However, the CBI group did not differ significantly from either CBL or TC groups. Additionally, below average students retained significantly more than average and above average students, and female students scored considerably higher than male students in the delayed post-test.

question: What are the differences in how well students learned math when using computers versus traditional teaching?

answer: The Computer Based Learning (CBL) group showed significantly different results from the Teacher Centered (TC) group in the delayed-posttest. The CBL group scored significantly higher in both achieving and retaining the mathematical content. The Computer Based Instruction (CBI) group did not differ significantly from either the CBL or TC groups. Additionally, below average students retained significantly more than average and above average students, and female students scored considerably higher than male students in the delayed-posttest.

question: What were the three different teaching approaches compared in a study about mathematics education in Pakistan, and how did they differ from each other?

answer: According to the study, below average students retained significantly more mathematical content than the average and above average students. The computer-based learning (CBL) approach, which utilized software, internet, email, chatting and online help, showed significantly better results for retention compared to traditional teaching methods. The study concluded that using CBL approach in teaching mathematics at secondary level in Pakistan can be encouraged for better achievement and retention of the subject.

question: How did the study implement Computer Based Learning compared to Computer Based Instruction in teaching mathematics?

answer: The study found significant differences in retention between different teaching approaches. The Computer Based Learning (CBL) group showed significantly better retention compared to the Teacher Centered (TC) group, as indicated by delayed-posttest scores. The Computer Based Instruction (CBI) group's performance didn't significantly differ from either CBL or TC groups. Notably, below average students retained significantly more content than average and above average students, and female students scored considerably higher than male students in the delayed-posttest.

question: What were the differential impacts of technology-enhanced pedagogical methodologies versus conventional instruction on mathematical comprehension retention among secondary education students?

answer: The post-hoc comparison using Tukey HSD test showed that the Computer Based Learning (CBL) group's mean score was significantly different from the Teacher Centered (TC) group on the delayed-posttest. The Computer Based Instruction (CBI) group did not differ significantly from either CBL or TC groups. The CBL group had significantly higher scores in both achieving and retaining the mathematical content.

question: In a comparison of computer-aided and traditional teaching methods, which approach demonstrated superior results for mathematics content retention, and what were the specific findings from the post-hoc analysis?

answer: The study employed a post test-only control group design with 63 heterogeneous students representing different ability levels and genders. Students were divided into three groups: Computer Based Instruction (CBI), Computer Based Learning (CBL), and Teacher Centered (TC) approach. All groups were taught the same topic 'Concept of Matrices' using their respective approaches. The assessment was conducted through researcher-made posttest and delayed-posttest, and data was analyzed using Two-way analysis of Covariance (ANCOVA) at 0.05 significance level.

question: What were the main findings about student retention when comparing computer-based learning (CBL) to traditional teaching methods?

answer: The study used Two-way analysis of Covariance (ANCOVA) from SPSS program to analyze the data, with significance levels set at 0.05. For achievement, there was no significant difference among scores of students taught through CBI, CBL, and TC approaches. The main effect of the groups and ability did not meet the 0.05 level of significance. However, for retention, the main effect comparison of groups was significant at 0.023, with CBL showing significantly better results than TC in the delayed-posttest.

context:
Tahir, Alyas Qadeer (2005) A COMPARATIVE STUDY OF THE EFFECT OF USE OF INFORMATION AND COMMUNICATION TECHNOLOGY IN VARIED TEACHING APPROACHES ON ACHIEVEMENT AND RETENTION OF STUDENTS OF MATHEMATICS. PhD thesis, Gomal University, D.I. Khan.
The purposed of this study was to compare and determine the more effective approach by used of information and communication technology (ICT) on achievement and retention of secondary school students of mathematics in Pakistan. The three approaches used for this study were, the computer based instruction(CBI), computer Based learning (CBL), and the teacher centered TC) approach. The research design was the post test-only control group design. Seventeen research questions were put in the study. Sample of the study were 63 students and was heterogeneous to provide representation across ability level and gender. The achievement and retention instruments were in the form of researchers, made posttedst and the delayed-posttest. The CBI and CBL boys and girls students (taught separately) were grouped as treatment group. The same topic of mathematics, ÃÂÃÂ¢ÃÂÃÂÃÂÃÂConcept of MatricesÃÂÃÂ¢ÃÂÃÂÃÂÃÂ was taught to all three groups. The CBI groups were taught through software-Educational Program of Gifted Youth (EPGY), developed by Stanford University of, USA. These groups were also supported by lectures, drill and practice and self-assessment opportunities. The CBL groups used the EPGY software and in addition they made sue of all possible resources of information and communication technology; internet, e-mail, chatting and on-line help. The TC groups were taught through traditional teaching techniques. Two-way analysis of Covariance (ANCOVA) procedure form SPSS program was adapted for the analysis of data of the study. Significant levels of all research hypothesis tested in this study were at the 0.05 levels. There was no significant difference among score of the students taught mathematics through CBI, CBL and TC approaches on achievement. The main effect of the groups and ability did not meet the 0.05 level of significance. Difference among the groupÃÂÃÂ¢ÃÂÃÂÃÂÃÂs retention of the students taught mathematics through CBI, CBL and TC approaches. The main effect of the ability did meet the 0.05 level of significance on delayed-posttest. The mian effect comparison of groups was also significant at 0.023. However, the main effect for group reach statistical significance on delayed-posttest. Post-hoc comparison using the Tukey HSD test indicated that the means score of delayed-posttest for the CBL group was significantly different from the TC group. The CBI group did not differ significantly from either of the CBL or TC groups. It was found that the CBL group had significantly scored higher in achieving as well as retaining the content of mathematics taught to them during the experiment. The blow average students retained significantly more than the average and above average students. The girls students overall scored considerably higher than the boys in delayed-posttest. It was concluded that the use of CBL approach in teaching of mathematics at secondary level in Pakistan can be encouraged for better achievement and retention of the subject which is one of the objective of teaching of mathematics at this stage.

question: In the comparative study of ICT teaching approaches, what statistical analysis methods were employed and what significance level was used for hypothesis testing?

answer: In comparing Computer Based Learning (CBL), Computer Based Instruction (CBI), and Teacher Centered (TC) approaches, while there was no significant difference in immediate achievement scores, the CBL group showed significantly higher retention scores compared to the TC group on delayed post-tests. The CBI group's performance did not significantly differ from either CBL or TC groups. Additionally, below average students demonstrated significantly better retention compared to average and above average students, and female students scored notably higher than male students in delayed post-tests.

As a result, the central government granted the Sultan of Yogyakarta the position of appointed governor of the DIY province - making it the only province in Indonesia where the governor is not elected directly by the people.

question: What makes Yogyakarta unique among Indonesian provinces in terms of its governance?

answer: Yogyakarta has a unique status in Indonesia where the Sultan of Yogyakarta serves as the appointed governor of the DIY province, making it the only province where the governor is not directly elected by the people. This arrangement stems from the sultanate's historical role during the Indonesian independence war, when Sultan Hamengkubuwono IX provided his enclave as the revolutionary capital city from 1946 to 1949 while Jakarta was under Dutch occupation. The current sultan, Hamengkubuwono X, maintains this position despite attempts by the central government to implement direct elections.
