Here’s a clue to my tools: if they’re not fun, they often won’t work. That’s why I’ll teach you my favorite games for playing with your kids that will get them thinking outside the box about their “mistakes,” which will then lead to them become self-starters and problem solvers.

P.S. “If I make a mistake or fail, I’ll be rejected.”
In Parenting That Empowers, you’ll learn how this belief gets formed and can create problems for your child later in life—and I’ll teach you how to keep your child from developing this painful result.Raising Them To Feel Capable

question: How has the ideal approach to handling mistakes changed as we moved from an industrial to an information-based society?

answer: Instead of treating mistakes as failures, parents can transform them into learning opportunities. For example, when a child spills milk, instead of scolding them, parents can say 'That's okay' and provide constructive guidance like 'take your little tiny hands and hold the container on both sides and very slowly tip it over.' This approach helps children feel that what happened was normal and that they themselves are normal too. Another strategy is to explicitly label mistakes as learning opportunities - one parent would say 'Ding! Ding! Ding! Learning opportunity!' whenever her child made a mistake. This teaches children that there are no such things as mistakes and failures, only outcomes that can be improved upon through different approaches.

One day when she was 9 years old, she looked at me and said, “It’s getting old mom, I got it.”
I know I had over done it, but I made my point. Neither of my kids feels that making mistakes or failing is bad.
And because of that, they are willing to go for it—they are willing to try things. When my daughter Britney did a half ironman, she didn’t know if she was going to finish. She said, “I am going to get out there, and I’m going to go for it.”
That’s what life is about. You know it’s about the journey, not the destination.
Going back to the scenario I described in the beginning, imagine now instead that mom comes over to you and says:
“You spilled the milk, that’s okay. You know, honey, you have little tiny hands, so I want you to take your little tiny hands and hold the container on both sides and very slowly tip it over.”
Can you see how the scene has shifted from a perceived failure to a learning opportunity?
Can you see how, as a child, you’d feel that what just happened was perfectly normal, and so you are, too?
I want to help teach your kids that there are no such things as mistakes and failures, they are only outcomes. If you don’t like the outcome, you can try something different.
So many times, parents are so busy that they miss little moments—such as the spilled milk—to make massive positive impact on their children. And because of this, they end up NEGATIVELY impacting children for life.
In my audio program Parenting That Empowers, I’ll give you many more examples, role-playing, and tools that will help you turn everyday parenting moments into opportunities to teach your child creativity and resourcefulness while building self-esteem and self-worth:Creative Kids = Innovative Adults
Here’s a clue to my tools: if they’re not fun, they often won’t work. That’s why I’ll teach you my favorite games for playing with your kids that will get them thinking outside the box about their “mistakes,” which will then lead to them become self-starters and problem solvers.
You’ll also learn how to nip negative beliefs in the bud, such as if your child comes home from school with a bad grade and concludes they’re stupid or not good at the subject. Just a few choice words from you will reorient your child’s thinking and get them to naturally strive for their best.Raising Kids Who Go For It
Bonus: when your child feels that mistakes are no big deal, he’ll also conclude that you love him—no matter what. And feeling loved just as we are is one of the most positive, powerful beliefs you can gift your child.
P.S. “If I make a mistake or fail, I’ll be rejected.”
In Parenting That Empowers, you’ll learn how this belief gets formed and can create problems for your child later in life—and I’ll teach you how to keep your child from developing this painful result.Raising Them To Feel Capable
question: How can parents turn a child spilling milk into a positive learning experience?

answer: Compulsory education was originally designed to prepare students for work in factories during the industrial society. This educational model has created a lasting problem where students emerge from school afraid of making mistakes and failing. This fear becomes particularly problematic in the modern information age, where innovation is crucial. For instance, even Google's co-founders have expressed concern about this issue, noting that people who are afraid to make mistakes or fail cannot effectively work at innovative companies. Success in the information age requires being innovative, which is impossible without the willingness to fail and learn from mistakes.
context:

This one belief is responsible for most troublesome behavior patterns in people, from fear of public speaking to procrastination to not being able to leave a job they hate to fear of taking chances.
Now, here’s the most important information for you as a parent: this fear starts in childhood. And it happens as a result of how parents react to us.
To give you a hint about what this belief could be, I want to give you a scenario:
Close your eyes and imagine you are a child and you just spilled your milk.
There it is—the whole contents of the glass crawling across the kitchen table. Now imagine that your mom comes in and says:
“What’s wrong with you? Can’t you watch what you’re doing?”
I have two questions for you:
How would you feel if your mom said this to you? What would you likely conclude?
Did you ever have a parent express disappointment and upset because you made a mistake like spilling your milk?
If so, I’m betting that in that moment, you felt really bad about yourself. You would have felt shame, and you would have concluded there must be something terribly wrong with you.
You would have thought, “Gee, I guess that if I spilled my milk, it must mean that I don’t know how to do things right.”
What else would you conclude?
How about that making mistakes is a very bad thing that needs to be avoided at all costs? How about that there will be negative consequences whenever you make a mistake?
Mom’s upset may have lasted no more than a minute, but can you see how the child’s belief can affect him for a lifetime?
Almost every person I’ve ever worked with has the belief that mistakes and failures are bad.
If you get yelled at every time you spill and drop something or break something or do something you are not supposed to do, is it any wonder that this is what you’re going to conclude?
Let’s take a closer look at how these beliefs got formed.
No matter what age you are, most of our parents were raised in an industrial society.
In fact, when we went from an agrarian society to an industrial society, school compulsory education was designed to teach kids how to work in a factory.
On the factory floor—and especially on an assembly line—if you screwed up and made a mistake, it was costly.

question: Could you explain the correlation between compulsory education's industrial origins and modern innovation challenges?

answer: Children who are consistently made to feel that mistakes and failures are negative can develop lifelong limiting beliefs that stifle their creativity and purpose. These beliefs can manifest in troublesome behavior patterns such as fear of public speaking, inability to leave unfulfilling jobs, and reluctance to take chances. They may also develop the belief that they'll be rejected if they make mistakes, which can prevent them from being innovative or trying new things. This fear of failure can particularly impact their academic performance, where they might conclude they're not good at a subject after receiving a bad grade, rather than seeing it as an opportunity for improvement.

question: What specific developmental impacts can occur in children who are consistently made to feel that mistakes and failures are negative?

answer: Mistakes and failures should be reframed as learning opportunities rather than negative experiences. Instead of treating accidents or mistakes as failures, parents can turn them into teaching moments. For example, when a child spills milk, instead of expressing disappointment, a parent could say 'You spilled the milk, that's okay. You have little tiny hands, so take them and hold the container on both sides and very slowly tip it over.' This approach helps children feel that what happened was normal and teaches them that there are no failures, only outcomes that can be improved upon through different approaches.

This mindset helps children become willing to try new things and fosters creativity and resourcefulness while building self-esteem and self-worth.

No matter what age you are, most of our parents were raised in an industrial society.
In fact, when we went from an agrarian society to an industrial society, school compulsory education was designed to teach kids how to work in a factory.
On the factory floor—and especially on an assembly line—if you screwed up and made a mistake, it was costly.
But do we live in an industrial society anymore? Of course not, we live in an information age.
In an information age, can you be successful without being innovative? Can you invent an iPhone or almost anything you need today to live in the world?
The answer is no.
In fact, Google co-founders Sergey Brin and Larry Page have said they wished they could start their own school, because kids come out of school afraid to make mistakes and fail—and you can’t work for Google if you are afraid to make mistakes or fail!
You can’t be innovative if you’re afraid to fail. You certainly can’t cure a disease. You can’t do anything truly breakthrough that hasn’t been done before without failing.
If your children believe that making mistakes and failing are bad, they’ll forever feel stifled—creatively and purposefully.
It’s so important that we allow our children to fail and that we teach them that failures are learning opportunities.
When my daughter Blake was little, I didn’t want her to have the belief that mistakes and failures were bad, so every time she made a mistake or failed, I would say, “Ding! Ding! Ding! Learning opportunity!”
One day when she was 9 years old, she looked at me and said, “It’s getting old mom, I got it.”
I know I had over done it, but I made my point. Neither of my kids feels that making mistakes or failing is bad.
And because of that, they are willing to go for it—they are willing to try things. When my daughter Britney did a half ironman, she didn’t know if she was going to finish. She said, “I am going to get out there, and I’m going to go for it.”
That’s what life is about. You know it’s about the journey, not the destination.
Going back to the scenario I described in the beginning, imagine now instead that mom comes over to you and says:
“You spilled the milk, that’s okay. You know, honey, you have little tiny hands, so I want you to take your little tiny hands and hold the container on both sides and very slowly tip it over.”
Can you see how the scene has shifted from a perceived failure to a learning opportunity?
Can you see how, as a child, you’d feel that what just happened was perfectly normal, and so you are, too?
I want to help teach your kids that there are no such things as mistakes and failures, they are only outcomes. If you don’t like the outcome, you can try something different.
So many times, parents are so busy that they miss little moments—such as the spilled milk—to make massive positive impact on their children. And because of this, they end up NEGATIVELY impacting children for life.
In my audio program Parenting That Empowers, I’ll give you many more examples, role-playing, and tools that will help you turn everyday parenting moments into opportunities to teach your child creativity and resourcefulness while building self-esteem and self-worth:Creative Kids = Innovative Adults
Here’s a clue to my tools: if they’re not fun, they often won’t work. That’s why I’ll teach you my favorite games for playing with your kids that will get them thinking outside the box about their “mistakes,” which will then lead to them become self-starters and problem solvers.
You’ll also learn how to nip negative beliefs in the bud, such as if your child comes home from school with a bad grade and concludes they’re stupid or not good at the subject. Just a few choice words from you will reorient your child’s thinking and get them to naturally strive for their best.Raising Kids Who Go For It
Bonus: when your child feels that mistakes are no big deal, he’ll also conclude that you love him—no matter what. And feeling loved just as we are is one of the most positive, powerful beliefs you can gift your child.
P.S. “If I make a mistake or fail, I’ll be rejected.”
In Parenting That Empowers, you’ll learn how this belief gets formed and can create problems for your child later in life—and I’ll teach you how to keep your child from developing this painful result.Raising Them To Feel Capable

If so, I’m betting that in that moment, you felt really bad about yourself. You would have felt shame, and you would have concluded there must be something terribly wrong with you.
You would have thought, “Gee, I guess that if I spilled my milk, it must mean that I don’t know how to do things right.”
What else would you conclude?
How about that making mistakes is a very bad thing that needs to be avoided at all costs? How about that there will be negative consequences whenever you make a mistake?
Mom’s upset may have lasted no more than a minute, but can you see how the child’s belief can affect him for a lifetime?
Almost every person I’ve ever worked with has the belief that mistakes and failures are bad.
If you get yelled at every time you spill and drop something or break something or do something you are not supposed to do, is it any wonder that this is what you’re going to conclude?
Let’s take a closer look at how these beliefs got formed.
No matter what age you are, most of our parents were raised in an industrial society.
In fact, when we went from an agrarian society to an industrial society, school compulsory education was designed to teach kids how to work in a factory.
On the factory floor—and especially on an assembly line—if you screwed up and made a mistake, it was costly.
But do we live in an industrial society anymore? Of course not, we live in an information age.
In an information age, can you be successful without being innovative? Can you invent an iPhone or almost anything you need today to live in the world?
The answer is no.
In fact, Google co-founders Sergey Brin and Larry Page have said they wished they could start their own school, because kids come out of school afraid to make mistakes and fail—and you can’t work for Google if you are afraid to make mistakes or fail!
You can’t be innovative if you’re afraid to fail. You certainly can’t cure a disease. You can’t do anything truly breakthrough that hasn’t been done before without failing.
If your children believe that making mistakes and failing are bad, they’ll forever feel stifled—creatively and purposefully.
It’s so important that we allow our children to fail and that we teach them that failures are learning opportunities.
When my daughter Blake was little, I didn’t want her to have the belief that mistakes and failures were bad, so every time she made a mistake or failed, I would say, “Ding! Ding! Ding! Learning opportunity!”
One day when she was 9 years old, she looked at me and said, “It’s getting old mom, I got it.”
I know I had over done it, but I made my point. Neither of my kids feels that making mistakes or failing is bad.
And because of that, they are willing to go for it—they are willing to try things. When my daughter Britney did a half ironman, she didn’t know if she was going to finish. She said, “I am going to get out there, and I’m going to go for it.”
That’s what life is about. You know it’s about the journey, not the destination.
Going back to the scenario I described in the beginning, imagine now instead that mom comes over to you and says:
“You spilled the milk, that’s okay. You know, honey, you have little tiny hands, so I want you to take your little tiny hands and hold the container on both sides and very slowly tip it over.”
Can you see how the scene has shifted from a perceived failure to a learning opportunity?
Can you see how, as a child, you’d feel that what just happened was perfectly normal, and so you are, too?
I want to help teach your kids that there are no such things as mistakes and failures, they are only outcomes. If you don’t like the outcome, you can try something different.
So many times, parents are so busy that they miss little moments—such as the spilled milk—to make massive positive impact on their children. And because of this, they end up NEGATIVELY impacting children for life.
In my audio program Parenting That Empowers, I’ll give you many more examples, role-playing, and tools that will help you turn everyday parenting moments into opportunities to teach your child creativity and resourcefulness while building self-esteem and self-worth:Creative Kids = Innovative Adults
Here’s a clue to my tools: if they’re not fun, they often won’t work. That’s why I’ll teach you my favorite games for playing with your kids that will get them thinking outside the box about their “mistakes,” which will then lead to them become self-starters and problem solvers.
You’ll also learn how to nip negative beliefs in the bud, such as if your child comes home from school with a bad grade and concludes they’re stupid or not good at the subject. Just a few choice words from you will reorient your child’s thinking and get them to naturally strive for their best.Raising Kids Who Go For It
Bonus: when your child feels that mistakes are no big deal, he’ll also conclude that you love him—no matter what. And feeling loved just as we are is one of the most positive, powerful beliefs you can gift your child.
P.S. “If I make a mistake or fail, I’ll be rejected.”
In Parenting That Empowers, you’ll learn how this belief gets formed and can create problems for your child later in life—and I’ll teach you how to keep your child from developing this painful result.Raising Them To Feel Capable

context:
In my work helping people overcome limiting beliefs about themselves, I’ve found that there is specifically ONE common belief responsible for the negative patterns that get in the way of creating the life we want.
This one belief is responsible for most troublesome behavior patterns in people, from fear of public speaking to procrastination to not being able to leave a job they hate to fear of taking chances.
Now, here’s the most important information for you as a parent: this fear starts in childhood. And it happens as a result of how parents react to us.
To give you a hint about what this belief could be, I want to give you a scenario:
Close your eyes and imagine you are a child and you just spilled your milk.
There it is—the whole contents of the glass crawling across the kitchen table. Now imagine that your mom comes in and says:
“What’s wrong with you? Can’t you watch what you’re doing?”
I have two questions for you:
How would you feel if your mom said this to you? What would you likely conclude?
Did you ever have a parent express disappointment and upset because you made a mistake like spilling your milk?
If so, I’m betting that in that moment, you felt really bad about yourself. You would have felt shame, and you would have concluded there must be something terribly wrong with you.
You would have thought, “Gee, I guess that if I spilled my milk, it must mean that I don’t know how to do things right.”
What else would you conclude?
How about that making mistakes is a very bad thing that needs to be avoided at all costs? How about that there will be negative consequences whenever you make a mistake?
Mom’s upset may have lasted no more than a minute, but can you see how the child’s belief can affect him for a lifetime?
Almost every person I’ve ever worked with has the belief that mistakes and failures are bad.
If you get yelled at every time you spill and drop something or break something or do something you are not supposed to do, is it any wonder that this is what you’re going to conclude?
Let’s take a closer look at how these beliefs got formed.
No matter what age you are, most of our parents were raised in an industrial society.
In fact, when we went from an agrarian society to an industrial society, school compulsory education was designed to teach kids how to work in a factory.
On the factory floor—and especially on an assembly line—if you screwed up and made a mistake, it was costly.
But do we live in an industrial society anymore? Of course not, we live in an information age.
In an information age, can you be successful without being innovative? Can you invent an iPhone or almost anything you need today to live in the world?
The answer is no.
In fact, Google co-founders Sergey Brin and Larry Page have said they wished they could start their own school, because kids come out of school afraid to make mistakes and fail—and you can’t work for Google if you are afraid to make mistakes or fail!
You can’t be innovative if you’re afraid to fail. You certainly can’t cure a disease. You can’t do anything truly breakthrough that hasn’t been done before without failing.
If your children believe that making mistakes and failing are bad, they’ll forever feel stifled—creatively and purposefully.
It’s so important that we allow our children to fail and that we teach them that failures are learning opportunities.
When my daughter Blake was little, I didn’t want her to have the belief that mistakes and failures were bad, so every time she made a mistake or failed, I would say, “Ding! Ding! Ding! Learning opportunity!”
One day when she was 9 years old, she looked at me and said, “It’s getting old mom, I got it.”
I know I had over done it, but I made my point. Neither of my kids feels that making mistakes or failing is bad.
And because of that, they are willing to go for it—they are willing to try things. When my daughter Britney did a half ironman, she didn’t know if she was going to finish. She said, “I am going to get out there, and I’m going to go for it.”
That’s what life is about. You know it’s about the journey, not the destination.
Going back to the scenario I described in the beginning, imagine now instead that mom comes over to you and says:
“You spilled the milk, that’s okay. You know, honey, you have little tiny hands, so I want you to take your little tiny hands and hold the container on both sides and very slowly tip it over.”
Can you see how the scene has shifted from a perceived failure to a learning opportunity?
Can you see how, as a child, you’d feel that what just happened was perfectly normal, and so you are, too?
I want to help teach your kids that there are no such things as mistakes and failures, they are only outcomes. If you don’t like the outcome, you can try something different.
So many times, parents are so busy that they miss little moments—such as the spilled milk—to make massive positive impact on their children. And because of this, they end up NEGATIVELY impacting children for life.
In my audio program Parenting That Empowers, I’ll give you many more examples, role-playing, and tools that will help you turn everyday parenting moments into opportunities to teach your child creativity and resourcefulness while building self-esteem and self-worth:Creative Kids = Innovative Adults
Here’s a clue to my tools: if they’re not fun, they often won’t work. That’s why I’ll teach you my favorite games for playing with your kids that will get them thinking outside the box about their “mistakes,” which will then lead to them become self-starters and problem solvers.
You’ll also learn how to nip negative beliefs in the bud, such as if your child comes home from school with a bad grade and concludes they’re stupid or not good at the subject. Just a few choice words from you will reorient your child’s thinking and get them to naturally strive for their best.Raising Kids Who Go For It
Bonus: when your child feels that mistakes are no big deal, he’ll also conclude that you love him—no matter what. And feeling loved just as we are is one of the most positive, powerful beliefs you can gift your child.
P.S. “If I make a mistake or fail, I’ll be rejected.”
In Parenting That Empowers, you’ll learn how this belief gets formed and can create problems for your child later in life—and I’ll teach you how to keep your child from developing this painful result.Raising Them To Feel Capable

This approach helps children feel that what happened is normal and teaches them that there are no such things as mistakes and failures - only outcomes that can be improved upon through different approaches.

question: What pedagogical strategies can transform failure experiences into developmental opportunities?

This approach helps children feel normal and develop a healthy attitude toward mistakes.

In Parenting That Empowers, you’ll learn how this belief gets formed and can create problems for your child later in life—and I’ll teach you how to keep your child from developing this painful result.Raising Them To Feel Capable
question: How does the fear of making mistakes impact innovation in the modern information age economy?

answer: In the industrial age, especially on factory floors and assembly lines, making mistakes was very costly and needed to be avoided. However, in today's information age, being afraid of mistakes can be detrimental because innovation is essential. You cannot create breakthrough achievements or invent new things like the iPhone without being willing to fail and make mistakes. Success in the information age requires innovation, which inherently involves taking risks and learning from failures.
context:
In my work helping people overcome limiting beliefs about themselves, I’ve found that there is specifically ONE common belief responsible for the negative patterns that get in the way of creating the life we want.
This one belief is responsible for most troublesome behavior patterns in people, from fear of public speaking to procrastination to not being able to leave a job they hate to fear of taking chances.
Now, here’s the most important information for you as a parent: this fear starts in childhood. And it happens as a result of how parents react to us.
To give you a hint about what this belief could be, I want to give you a scenario:
Close your eyes and imagine you are a child and you just spilled your milk.
There it is—the whole contents of the glass crawling across the kitchen table. Now imagine that your mom comes in and says:
“What’s wrong with you? Can’t you watch what you’re doing?”
I have two questions for you:
How would you feel if your mom said this to you? What would you likely conclude?
Did you ever have a parent express disappointment and upset because you made a mistake like spilling your milk?
If so, I’m betting that in that moment, you felt really bad about yourself. You would have felt shame, and you would have concluded there must be something terribly wrong with you.
You would have thought, “Gee, I guess that if I spilled my milk, it must mean that I don’t know how to do things right.”
What else would you conclude?
How about that making mistakes is a very bad thing that needs to be avoided at all costs? How about that there will be negative consequences whenever you make a mistake?
Mom’s upset may have lasted no more than a minute, but can you see how the child’s belief can affect him for a lifetime?
Almost every person I’ve ever worked with has the belief that mistakes and failures are bad.
If you get yelled at every time you spill and drop something or break something or do something you are not supposed to do, is it any wonder that this is what you’re going to conclude?
Let’s take a closer look at how these beliefs got formed.
No matter what age you are, most of our parents were raised in an industrial society.
In fact, when we went from an agrarian society to an industrial society, school compulsory education was designed to teach kids how to work in a factory.
On the factory floor—and especially on an assembly line—if you screwed up and made a mistake, it was costly.
But do we live in an industrial society anymore? Of course not, we live in an information age.
In an information age, can you be successful without being innovative? Can you invent an iPhone or almost anything you need today to live in the world?
The answer is no.
In fact, Google co-founders Sergey Brin and Larry Page have said they wished they could start their own school, because kids come out of school afraid to make mistakes and fail—and you can’t work for Google if you are afraid to make mistakes or fail!
You can’t be innovative if you’re afraid to fail. You certainly can’t cure a disease. You can’t do anything truly breakthrough that hasn’t been done before without failing.
If your children believe that making mistakes and failing are bad, they’ll forever feel stifled—creatively and purposefully.
It’s so important that we allow our children to fail and that we teach them that failures are learning opportunities.
When my daughter Blake was little, I didn’t want her to have the belief that mistakes and failures were bad, so every time she made a mistake or failed, I would say, “Ding! Ding! Ding! Learning opportunity!”
One day when she was 9 years old, she looked at me and said, “It’s getting old mom, I got it.”
I know I had over done it, but I made my point. Neither of my kids feels that making mistakes or failing is bad.
And because of that, they are willing to go for it—they are willing to try things. When my daughter Britney did a half ironman, she didn’t know if she was going to finish. She said, “I am going to get out there, and I’m going to go for it.”
That’s what life is about. You know it’s about the journey, not the destination.
Going back to the scenario I described in the beginning, imagine now instead that mom comes over to you and says:
“You spilled the milk, that’s okay. You know, honey, you have little tiny hands, so I want you to take your little tiny hands and hold the container on both sides and very slowly tip it over.”
Can you see how the scene has shifted from a perceived failure to a learning opportunity?
Can you see how, as a child, you’d feel that what just happened was perfectly normal, and so you are, too?
I want to help teach your kids that there are no such things as mistakes and failures, they are only outcomes. If you don’t like the outcome, you can try something different.
So many times, parents are so busy that they miss little moments—such as the spilled milk—to make massive positive impact on their children. And because of this, they end up NEGATIVELY impacting children for life.
In my audio program Parenting That Empowers, I’ll give you many more examples, role-playing, and tools that will help you turn everyday parenting moments into opportunities to teach your child creativity and resourcefulness while building self-esteem and self-worth:Creative Kids = Innovative Adults
Here’s a clue to my tools: if they’re not fun, they often won’t work. That’s why I’ll teach you my favorite games for playing with your kids that will get them thinking outside the box about their “mistakes,” which will then lead to them become self-starters and problem solvers.
You’ll also learn how to nip negative beliefs in the bud, such as if your child comes home from school with a bad grade and concludes they’re stupid or not good at the subject. Just a few choice words from you will reorient your child’s thinking and get them to naturally strive for their best.Raising Kids Who Go For It
Bonus: when your child feels that mistakes are no big deal, he’ll also conclude that you love him—no matter what. And feeling loved just as we are is one of the most positive, powerful beliefs you can gift your child.
P.S. “If I make a mistake or fail, I’ll be rejected.”
In Parenting That Empowers, you’ll learn how this belief gets formed and can create problems for your child later in life—and I’ll teach you how to keep your child from developing this painful result.Raising Them To Feel Capable

question: How does the fear of making mistakes develop during childhood?

answer: When parents react negatively to accidents like spilled milk by saying things like 'What's wrong with you?', children often develop shame and conclude something is wrong with them. This leads to the belief that mistakes and failures are bad and must be avoided at all costs. This belief formed in childhood can affect someone for life and is responsible for many troublesome behavior patterns including fear of public speaking, procrastination, inability to leave unfulfilling jobs, and fear of taking chances.

question: From a developmental psychology perspective, how can parental reactions to accidents shape a child's long-term behavioral patterns?

answer: One effective strategy is to reframe mistakes as learning opportunities. This approach helps children see mistakes as normal occurrences and opportunities to learn, rather than failures. It's important to teach children that there are no such things as mistakes and failures - only outcomes that can be adjusted if needed.

In my audio program Parenting That Empowers, I’ll give you many more examples, role-playing, and tools that will help you turn everyday parenting moments into opportunities to teach your child creativity and resourcefulness while building self-esteem and self-worth:Creative Kids = Innovative Adults
Here’s a clue to my tools: if they’re not fun, they often won’t work. That’s why I’ll teach you my favorite games for playing with your kids that will get them thinking outside the box about their “mistakes,” which will then lead to them become self-starters and problem solvers.
You’ll also learn how to nip negative beliefs in the bud, such as if your child comes home from school with a bad grade and concludes they’re stupid or not good at the subject. Just a few choice words from you will reorient your child’s thinking and get them to naturally strive for their best.Raising Kids Who Go For It
Bonus: when your child feels that mistakes are no big deal, he’ll also conclude that you love him—no matter what. And feeling loved just as we are is one of the most positive, powerful beliefs you can gift your child.
P.S. “If I make a mistake or fail, I’ll be rejected.”
In Parenting That Empowers, you’ll learn how this belief gets formed and can create problems for your child later in life—and I’ll teach you how to keep your child from developing this painful result.Raising Them To Feel Capable
question: What is the key reason why many people struggle with public speaking and taking chances in life?

answer: Google co-founders Sergey Brin and Larry Page wish they could start their own school because kids come out of school afraid to make mistakes and fail. This is problematic because you can't work at Google if you're afraid to make mistakes or fail, as innovation requires being willing to fail.
context:
In my work helping people overcome limiting beliefs about themselves, I’ve found that there is specifically ONE common belief responsible for the negative patterns that get in the way of creating the life we want.
This one belief is responsible for most troublesome behavior patterns in people, from fear of public speaking to procrastination to not being able to leave a job they hate to fear of taking chances.
Now, here’s the most important information for you as a parent: this fear starts in childhood. And it happens as a result of how parents react to us.
To give you a hint about what this belief could be, I want to give you a scenario:
Close your eyes and imagine you are a child and you just spilled your milk.
There it is—the whole contents of the glass crawling across the kitchen table. Now imagine that your mom comes in and says:
“What’s wrong with you? Can’t you watch what you’re doing?”
I have two questions for you:
How would you feel if your mom said this to you? What would you likely conclude?
Did you ever have a parent express disappointment and upset because you made a mistake like spilling your milk?
If so, I’m betting that in that moment, you felt really bad about yourself. You would have felt shame, and you would have concluded there must be something terribly wrong with you.
You would have thought, “Gee, I guess that if I spilled my milk, it must mean that I don’t know how to do things right.”
What else would you conclude?
How about that making mistakes is a very bad thing that needs to be avoided at all costs? How about that there will be negative consequences whenever you make a mistake?
Mom’s upset may have lasted no more than a minute, but can you see how the child’s belief can affect him for a lifetime?
Almost every person I’ve ever worked with has the belief that mistakes and failures are bad.
If you get yelled at every time you spill and drop something or break something or do something you are not supposed to do, is it any wonder that this is what you’re going to conclude?
Let’s take a closer look at how these beliefs got formed.
No matter what age you are, most of our parents were raised in an industrial society.
In fact, when we went from an agrarian society to an industrial society, school compulsory education was designed to teach kids how to work in a factory.
On the factory floor—and especially on an assembly line—if you screwed up and made a mistake, it was costly.
But do we live in an industrial society anymore? Of course not, we live in an information age.
In an information age, can you be successful without being innovative? Can you invent an iPhone or almost anything you need today to live in the world?
The answer is no.
In fact, Google co-founders Sergey Brin and Larry Page have said they wished they could start their own school, because kids come out of school afraid to make mistakes and fail—and you can’t work for Google if you are afraid to make mistakes or fail!
You can’t be innovative if you’re afraid to fail. You certainly can’t cure a disease. You can’t do anything truly breakthrough that hasn’t been done before without failing.
If your children believe that making mistakes and failing are bad, they’ll forever feel stifled—creatively and purposefully.
It’s so important that we allow our children to fail and that we teach them that failures are learning opportunities.
When my daughter Blake was little, I didn’t want her to have the belief that mistakes and failures were bad, so every time she made a mistake or failed, I would say, “Ding! Ding! Ding! Learning opportunity!”
One day when she was 9 years old, she looked at me and said, “It’s getting old mom, I got it.”
I know I had over done it, but I made my point. Neither of my kids feels that making mistakes or failing is bad.
And because of that, they are willing to go for it—they are willing to try things. When my daughter Britney did a half ironman, she didn’t know if she was going to finish. She said, “I am going to get out there, and I’m going to go for it.”
That’s what life is about. You know it’s about the journey, not the destination.
Going back to the scenario I described in the beginning, imagine now instead that mom comes over to you and says:
“You spilled the milk, that’s okay. You know, honey, you have little tiny hands, so I want you to take your little tiny hands and hold the container on both sides and very slowly tip it over.”
Can you see how the scene has shifted from a perceived failure to a learning opportunity?
Can you see how, as a child, you’d feel that what just happened was perfectly normal, and so you are, too?
I want to help teach your kids that there are no such things as mistakes and failures, they are only outcomes. If you don’t like the outcome, you can try something different.
So many times, parents are so busy that they miss little moments—such as the spilled milk—to make massive positive impact on their children. And because of this, they end up NEGATIVELY impacting children for life.
In my audio program Parenting That Empowers, I’ll give you many more examples, role-playing, and tools that will help you turn everyday parenting moments into opportunities to teach your child creativity and resourcefulness while building self-esteem and self-worth:Creative Kids = Innovative Adults
Here’s a clue to my tools: if they’re not fun, they often won’t work. That’s why I’ll teach you my favorite games for playing with your kids that will get them thinking outside the box about their “mistakes,” which will then lead to them become self-starters and problem solvers.
You’ll also learn how to nip negative beliefs in the bud, such as if your child comes home from school with a bad grade and concludes they’re stupid or not good at the subject. Just a few choice words from you will reorient your child’s thinking and get them to naturally strive for their best.Raising Kids Who Go For It
Bonus: when your child feels that mistakes are no big deal, he’ll also conclude that you love him—no matter what. And feeling loved just as we are is one of the most positive, powerful beliefs you can gift your child.
P.S. “If I make a mistake or fail, I’ll be rejected.”
In Parenting That Empowers, you’ll learn how this belief gets formed and can create problems for your child later in life—and I’ll teach you how to keep your child from developing this painful result.Raising Them To Feel Capable

question: In the context of child development, what is the recommended parental response to children's mistakes to foster positive learning?

answer: Parents can help by treating mistakes as learning opportunities rather than failures. For example, when a child spills milk, instead of saying 'What's wrong with you?', parents should offer constructive guidance like showing them how to hold the container properly. It's crucial to teach children that there are no such things as mistakes and failures - only outcomes that can be improved upon. This approach helps children feel normal when they make mistakes and encourages them to try new things without fear. Parents should also make learning from mistakes fun, as this helps children become self-starters and problem solvers.

That’s what life is about. You know it’s about the journey, not the destination.
Going back to the scenario I described in the beginning, imagine now instead that mom comes over to you and says:

Can you see how the scene has shifted from a perceived failure to a learning opportunity?
Can you see how, as a child, you’d feel that what just happened was perfectly normal, and so you are, too?
I want to help teach your kids that there are no such things as mistakes and failures, they are only outcomes. If you don’t like the outcome, you can try something different.
So many times, parents are so busy that they miss little moments—such as the spilled milk—to make massive positive impact on their children. And because of this, they end up NEGATIVELY impacting children for life.
In my audio program Parenting That Empowers, I’ll give you many more examples, role-playing, and tools that will help you turn everyday parenting moments into opportunities to teach your child creativity and resourcefulness while building self-esteem and self-worth:Creative Kids = Innovative Adults
Here’s a clue to my tools: if they’re not fun, they often won’t work. That’s why I’ll teach you my favorite games for playing with your kids that will get them thinking outside the box about their “mistakes,” which will then lead to them become self-starters and problem solvers.
You’ll also learn how to nip negative beliefs in the bud, such as if your child comes home from school with a bad grade and concludes they’re stupid or not good at the subject. Just a few choice words from you will reorient your child’s thinking and get them to naturally strive for their best.Raising Kids Who Go For It
Bonus: when your child feels that mistakes are no big deal, he’ll also conclude that you love him—no matter what. And feeling loved just as we are is one of the most positive, powerful beliefs you can gift your child.
P.S. “If I make a mistake or fail, I’ll be rejected.”
In Parenting That Empowers, you’ll learn how this belief gets formed and can create problems for your child later in life—and I’ll teach you how to keep your child from developing this painful result.Raising Them To Feel Capable
question: How can parents help their kids develop a healthy attitude towards making mistakes?

answer: When children are consistently criticized for mistakes like spilling or breaking things, they develop the belief that mistakes and failures are bad and must be avoided at all costs. This belief can lead to various troublesome behavior patterns later in life, including fear of public speaking, procrastination, inability to leave unfulfilling jobs, and fear of taking chances. This is particularly problematic in today's information age, where innovation and willingness to fail are crucial for success. Companies like Google specifically look for people who aren't afraid to make mistakes, as breakthrough innovations cannot happen without failing and learning from those failures.
context:
In my work helping people overcome limiting beliefs about themselves, I’ve found that there is specifically ONE common belief responsible for the negative patterns that get in the way of creating the life we want.
This one belief is responsible for most troublesome behavior patterns in people, from fear of public speaking to procrastination to not being able to leave a job they hate to fear of taking chances.
Now, here’s the most important information for you as a parent: this fear starts in childhood. And it happens as a result of how parents react to us.
To give you a hint about what this belief could be, I want to give you a scenario:
Close your eyes and imagine you are a child and you just spilled your milk.
There it is—the whole contents of the glass crawling across the kitchen table. Now imagine that your mom comes in and says:
“What’s wrong with you? Can’t you watch what you’re doing?”
I have two questions for you:
How would you feel if your mom said this to you? What would you likely conclude?
Did you ever have a parent express disappointment and upset because you made a mistake like spilling your milk?
If so, I’m betting that in that moment, you felt really bad about yourself. You would have felt shame, and you would have concluded there must be something terribly wrong with you.
You would have thought, “Gee, I guess that if I spilled my milk, it must mean that I don’t know how to do things right.”
What else would you conclude?
How about that making mistakes is a very bad thing that needs to be avoided at all costs? How about that there will be negative consequences whenever you make a mistake?

The belief that mistakes and failures are bad, formed during childhood, can stifle creativity and purpose in adulthood. That's why it's crucial for parents to handle these formative moments carefully to prevent negative beliefs from developing.

But do we live in an industrial society anymore? Of course not, we live in an information age.
In an information age, can you be successful without being innovative? Can you invent an iPhone or almost anything you need today to live in the world?
The answer is no.
In fact, Google co-founders Sergey Brin and Larry Page have said they wished they could start their own school, because kids come out of school afraid to make mistakes and fail—and you can’t work for Google if you are afraid to make mistakes or fail!
You can’t be innovative if you’re afraid to fail. You certainly can’t cure a disease. You can’t do anything truly breakthrough that hasn’t been done before without failing.
If your children believe that making mistakes and failing are bad, they’ll forever feel stifled—creatively and purposefully.
It’s so important that we allow our children to fail and that we teach them that failures are learning opportunities.
When my daughter Blake was little, I didn’t want her to have the belief that mistakes and failures were bad, so every time she made a mistake or failed, I would say, “Ding! Ding! Ding! Learning opportunity!”
One day when she was 9 years old, she looked at me and said, “It’s getting old mom, I got it.”
I know I had over done it, but I made my point. Neither of my kids feels that making mistakes or failing is bad.
And because of that, they are willing to go for it—they are willing to try things. When my daughter Britney did a half ironman, she didn’t know if she was going to finish. She said, “I am going to get out there, and I’m going to go for it.”
That’s what life is about. You know it’s about the journey, not the destination.
Going back to the scenario I described in the beginning, imagine now instead that mom comes over to you and says:
“You spilled the milk, that’s okay. You know, honey, you have little tiny hands, so I want you to take your little tiny hands and hold the container on both sides and very slowly tip it over.”
Can you see how the scene has shifted from a perceived failure to a learning opportunity?
Can you see how, as a child, you’d feel that what just happened was perfectly normal, and so you are, too?
I want to help teach your kids that there are no such things as mistakes and failures, they are only outcomes. If you don’t like the outcome, you can try something different.
So many times, parents are so busy that they miss little moments—such as the spilled milk—to make massive positive impact on their children. And because of this, they end up NEGATIVELY impacting children for life.
In my audio program Parenting That Empowers, I’ll give you many more examples, role-playing, and tools that will help you turn everyday parenting moments into opportunities to teach your child creativity and resourcefulness while building self-esteem and self-worth:Creative Kids = Innovative Adults
Here’s a clue to my tools: if they’re not fun, they often won’t work. That’s why I’ll teach you my favorite games for playing with your kids that will get them thinking outside the box about their “mistakes,” which will then lead to them become self-starters and problem solvers.
You’ll also learn how to nip negative beliefs in the bud, such as if your child comes home from school with a bad grade and concludes they’re stupid or not good at the subject. Just a few choice words from you will reorient your child’s thinking and get them to naturally strive for their best.Raising Kids Who Go For It
Bonus: when your child feels that mistakes are no big deal, he’ll also conclude that you love him—no matter what. And feeling loved just as we are is one of the most positive, powerful beliefs you can gift your child.
P.S. “If I make a mistake or fail, I’ll be rejected.”
In Parenting That Empowers, you’ll learn how this belief gets formed and can create problems for your child later in life—and I’ll teach you how to keep your child from developing this painful result.Raising Them To Feel Capable

question: What pedagogical methods can be employed to foster a positive attitude toward mistakes in child development?

answer: Traditional industrial-era education, designed for factory work, taught that mistakes were costly and should be avoided. This approach is problematic in today's information age, where innovation is crucial. For example, Google co-founders have noted that students emerge from school afraid to make mistakes and fail, which is incompatible with innovative work. You can't be truly innovative, invent breakthrough products, or cure diseases if you're afraid to fail. Success in the modern information age requires being willing to take risks and view failures as learning opportunities rather than negative outcomes.

answer: Early childhood experiences, particularly how parents react to mistakes and failures, significantly impact future risk-taking behavior. When children are taught that failures are learning opportunities rather than negative events, they become more willing to try new things and take chances later in life. Conversely, if children develop the belief that mistakes are bad, they'll feel perpetually stifled both creatively and purposefully throughout their lives.

question: How does the historical transition from industrial to information age society affect the importance of embracing failure in education and innovation?

answer: Reframing mistakes as learning opportunities rather than failures has significant developmental benefits. When parents respond constructively to mistakes - for instance, by saying 'that's okay' and providing guidance when a child spills milk, rather than expressing disappointment - children learn that mistakes are normal and natural. This approach helps children feel confident to try new things and 'go for it' without fear of failure. They learn to see mistakes not as failures but as outcomes, understanding that if they don't like an outcome, they can try something different. This mindset helps build self-esteem and self-worth, while developing creativity and resourcefulness. Additionally, when children feel mistakes are acceptable, they conclude that they are loved unconditionally.

“What’s wrong with you? Can’t you watch what you’re doing?”
I have two questions for you:
How would you feel if your mom said this to you? What would you likely conclude?
Did you ever have a parent express disappointment and upset because you made a mistake like spilling your milk?
If so, I’m betting that in that moment, you felt really bad about yourself. You would have felt shame, and you would have concluded there must be something terribly wrong with you.
You would have thought, “Gee, I guess that if I spilled my milk, it must mean that I don’t know how to do things right.”
What else would you conclude?
How about that making mistakes is a very bad thing that needs to be avoided at all costs? How about that there will be negative consequences whenever you make a mistake?
Mom’s upset may have lasted no more than a minute, but can you see how the child’s belief can affect him for a lifetime?
Almost every person I’ve ever worked with has the belief that mistakes and failures are bad.
If you get yelled at every time you spill and drop something or break something or do something you are not supposed to do, is it any wonder that this is what you’re going to conclude?
Let’s take a closer look at how these beliefs got formed.
No matter what age you are, most of our parents were raised in an industrial society.
In fact, when we went from an agrarian society to an industrial society, school compulsory education was designed to teach kids how to work in a factory.
On the factory floor—and especially on an assembly line—if you screwed up and made a mistake, it was costly.
But do we live in an industrial society anymore? Of course not, we live in an information age.
In an information age, can you be successful without being innovative? Can you invent an iPhone or almost anything you need today to live in the world?
The answer is no.
In fact, Google co-founders Sergey Brin and Larry Page have said they wished they could start their own school, because kids come out of school afraid to make mistakes and fail—and you can’t work for Google if you are afraid to make mistakes or fail!
You can’t be innovative if you’re afraid to fail. You certainly can’t cure a disease. You can’t do anything truly breakthrough that hasn’t been done before without failing.
If your children believe that making mistakes and failing are bad, they’ll forever feel stifled—creatively and purposefully.
It’s so important that we allow our children to fail and that we teach them that failures are learning opportunities.
When my daughter Blake was little, I didn’t want her to have the belief that mistakes and failures were bad, so every time she made a mistake or failed, I would say, “Ding! Ding! Ding! Learning opportunity!”
One day when she was 9 years old, she looked at me and said, “It’s getting old mom, I got it.”
I know I had over done it, but I made my point. Neither of my kids feels that making mistakes or failing is bad.
And because of that, they are willing to go for it—they are willing to try things. When my daughter Britney did a half ironman, she didn’t know if she was going to finish. She said, “I am going to get out there, and I’m going to go for it.”
That’s what life is about. You know it’s about the journey, not the destination.
Going back to the scenario I described in the beginning, imagine now instead that mom comes over to you and says:
“You spilled the milk, that’s okay. You know, honey, you have little tiny hands, so I want you to take your little tiny hands and hold the container on both sides and very slowly tip it over.”
Can you see how the scene has shifted from a perceived failure to a learning opportunity?
Can you see how, as a child, you’d feel that what just happened was perfectly normal, and so you are, too?
I want to help teach your kids that there are no such things as mistakes and failures, they are only outcomes. If you don’t like the outcome, you can try something different.
So many times, parents are so busy that they miss little moments—such as the spilled milk—to make massive positive impact on their children. And because of this, they end up NEGATIVELY impacting children for life.
In my audio program Parenting That Empowers, I’ll give you many more examples, role-playing, and tools that will help you turn everyday parenting moments into opportunities to teach your child creativity and resourcefulness while building self-esteem and self-worth:Creative Kids = Innovative Adults
Here’s a clue to my tools: if they’re not fun, they often won’t work. That’s why I’ll teach you my favorite games for playing with your kids that will get them thinking outside the box about their “mistakes,” which will then lead to them become self-starters and problem solvers.
You’ll also learn how to nip negative beliefs in the bud, such as if your child comes home from school with a bad grade and concludes they’re stupid or not good at the subject. Just a few choice words from you will reorient your child’s thinking and get them to naturally strive for their best.Raising Kids Who Go For It
Bonus: when your child feels that mistakes are no big deal, he’ll also conclude that you love him—no matter what. And feeling loved just as we are is one of the most positive, powerful beliefs you can gift your child.
P.S. “If I make a mistake or fail, I’ll be rejected.”
In Parenting That Empowers, you’ll learn how this belief gets formed and can create problems for your child later in life—and I’ll teach you how to keep your child from developing this painful result.Raising Them To Feel Capable
question: What are the developmental implications of reframing childhood mistakes as learning opportunities rather than failures?

answer: These struggles stem from a fundamental belief that develops in childhood - that making mistakes and failing are bad things that should be avoided. This belief forms when parents react negatively to children's mistakes, like getting upset when they spill something. When children are consistently criticized for their mistakes, they internalize the idea that errors have negative consequences, which can affect them for their entire life and manifest in behaviors like fear of public speaking or reluctance to take chances.

question: What pedagogical techniques can be implemented to transform children's mistakes into positive learning experiences?

answer: Companies like Google specifically look for employees who aren't afraid to make mistakes or fail because in today's information age, innovation is crucial. As stated by Google co-founders Sergey Brin and Larry Page, they wish they could start their own school because students come out of education afraid to make mistakes - which makes them unsuitable for working at Google. You can't be innovative if you're afraid to fail, whether it's inventing new technologies or making breakthrough discoveries. This is very different from the industrial age, where mistakes on factory floors were costly.
context:
In my work helping people overcome limiting beliefs about themselves, I’ve found that there is specifically ONE common belief responsible for the negative patterns that get in the way of creating the life we want.
This one belief is responsible for most troublesome behavior patterns in people, from fear of public speaking to procrastination to not being able to leave a job they hate to fear of taking chances.
Now, here’s the most important information for you as a parent: this fear starts in childhood. And it happens as a result of how parents react to us.
To give you a hint about what this belief could be, I want to give you a scenario:
Close your eyes and imagine you are a child and you just spilled your milk.
There it is—the whole contents of the glass crawling across the kitchen table. Now imagine that your mom comes in and says:
“What’s wrong with you? Can’t you watch what you’re doing?”
I have two questions for you:
How would you feel if your mom said this to you? What would you likely conclude?
Did you ever have a parent express disappointment and upset because you made a mistake like spilling your milk?
If so, I’m betting that in that moment, you felt really bad about yourself. You would have felt shame, and you would have concluded there must be something terribly wrong with you.
You would have thought, “Gee, I guess that if I spilled my milk, it must mean that I don’t know how to do things right.”
What else would you conclude?
How about that making mistakes is a very bad thing that needs to be avoided at all costs? How about that there will be negative consequences whenever you make a mistake?
Mom’s upset may have lasted no more than a minute, but can you see how the child’s belief can affect him for a lifetime?
Almost every person I’ve ever worked with has the belief that mistakes and failures are bad.
If you get yelled at every time you spill and drop something or break something or do something you are not supposed to do, is it any wonder that this is what you’re going to conclude?
Let’s take a closer look at how these beliefs got formed.
No matter what age you are, most of our parents were raised in an industrial society.
In fact, when we went from an agrarian society to an industrial society, school compulsory education was designed to teach kids how to work in a factory.
On the factory floor—and especially on an assembly line—if you screwed up and made a mistake, it was costly.
But do we live in an industrial society anymore? Of course not, we live in an information age.
In an information age, can you be successful without being innovative? Can you invent an iPhone or almost anything you need today to live in the world?
The answer is no.
In fact, Google co-founders Sergey Brin and Larry Page have said they wished they could start their own school, because kids come out of school afraid to make mistakes and fail—and you can’t work for Google if you are afraid to make mistakes or fail!
You can’t be innovative if you’re afraid to fail. You certainly can’t cure a disease. You can’t do anything truly breakthrough that hasn’t been done before without failing.
If your children believe that making mistakes and failing are bad, they’ll forever feel stifled—creatively and purposefully.
It’s so important that we allow our children to fail and that we teach them that failures are learning opportunities.
When my daughter Blake was little, I didn’t want her to have the belief that mistakes and failures were bad, so every time she made a mistake or failed, I would say, “Ding! Ding! Ding! Learning opportunity!”
One day when she was 9 years old, she looked at me and said, “It’s getting old mom, I got it.”
I know I had over done it, but I made my point. Neither of my kids feels that making mistakes or failing is bad.
And because of that, they are willing to go for it—they are willing to try things. When my daughter Britney did a half ironman, she didn’t know if she was going to finish. She said, “I am going to get out there, and I’m going to go for it.”
That’s what life is about. You know it’s about the journey, not the destination.
Going back to the scenario I described in the beginning, imagine now instead that mom comes over to you and says:
“You spilled the milk, that’s okay. You know, honey, you have little tiny hands, so I want you to take your little tiny hands and hold the container on both sides and very slowly tip it over.”
Can you see how the scene has shifted from a perceived failure to a learning opportunity?
Can you see how, as a child, you’d feel that what just happened was perfectly normal, and so you are, too?
I want to help teach your kids that there are no such things as mistakes and failures, they are only outcomes. If you don’t like the outcome, you can try something different.
So many times, parents are so busy that they miss little moments—such as the spilled milk—to make massive positive impact on their children. And because of this, they end up NEGATIVELY impacting children for life.
In my audio program Parenting That Empowers, I’ll give you many more examples, role-playing, and tools that will help you turn everyday parenting moments into opportunities to teach your child creativity and resourcefulness while building self-esteem and self-worth:Creative Kids = Innovative Adults
Here’s a clue to my tools: if they’re not fun, they often won’t work. That’s why I’ll teach you my favorite games for playing with your kids that will get them thinking outside the box about their “mistakes,” which will then lead to them become self-starters and problem solvers.
You’ll also learn how to nip negative beliefs in the bud, such as if your child comes home from school with a bad grade and concludes they’re stupid or not good at the subject. Just a few choice words from you will reorient your child’s thinking and get them to naturally strive for their best.Raising Kids Who Go For It
Bonus: when your child feels that mistakes are no big deal, he’ll also conclude that you love him—no matter what. And feeling loved just as we are is one of the most positive, powerful beliefs you can gift your child.
P.S. “If I make a mistake or fail, I’ll be rejected.”
In Parenting That Empowers, you’ll learn how this belief gets formed and can create problems for your child later in life—and I’ll teach you how to keep your child from developing this painful result.Raising Them To Feel Capable
question: Why do some successful tech companies actually want employees who are willing to make mistakes?
